EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-dawn severe thunderstorms developed across SE GA on the northern side of a lifting warm front across N Florida. Elevated instability and strong dynamics coincided over SE GA ahead of a surface low that track eastward from the FL panhandle toward the local forecast area through the morning hours. Through the late morning hours, the shortwave trough aloft lifted northeast toward the mid-Atlantic coastline into the afternoon. This pulled a very strong low level jet of 50-70 kts NE as well, but continued to produce a favorable environment for severe storms through midday as the warm front lifted north across SE GA placing NE FL in the warm sector under strong dynamics for severe storm potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado briefly touched down near the eastern section of Marion Oaks, impacted Courtlandt Farm, Lyladaisical Ranch, crossed I-75 south, damaged the SECO power station just east of I-75 and damaged a home and several structures across the street from the power station along SW 16th Avenue before lifting up across the property fields.  A video was taking of the tornado crossing I-75. At the start of the event the tornado was rated an EF0 with peak winds near 85 near Courtlandt Farm and Lyladiasical Ranch. Damage in this area on the west side of I-75 included large hardwood trees with multiple branches blown down, fencing damaged, and cedar trees and southwoods damaged. The SECO power station had signficant damage to its utility structures. There were two wooden structures with more than 20% of their roofs uplifted. A nearby structure had fascades ripped off of it. A street sign was twisted and snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of strong upper level troughs pushed through the southeast states, resulting in another enhanced risk of severe storms from the Storm Prediction Center.  The tri-state area was hit fairly hard again, with numerous reports of wind damage documented along with several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Ashville Highway and also on Brock Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved through the region, showers and thunderstorms ahead of the front mostly remained below severe levels.  However, one tornado occurred in Gadsden county associated with a discrete supercell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down northeast of Gretna, FL in a field south of M&M Lane and moved northeast through a community with the final piece of damage being seen on Bainbridge highway. The majority of the damage was uprooted and snapped trees as well as some siding, roof and undercarriage damage on manufactured homes. The areas to the northeast and southwest were extensively surveyed but no additional damage was found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong speed shear preceded a potent storm system on February 6th. By early afternoon, widespread wind gusts of 40-47 mph from the SSW were reported across NE Florida through about 5 pm which caused tree damage and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient (non-thunderstorm) winds began during the late morning and continued through mid- afternoon across Alachua County. At 12:40 pm a mesonet sensor near the University of Florida measured a gust of 47 mph from the SSW.  At 12:53 pm the Gainesville Airport ASOS measured a wind gust of 40 mph which broke the daily record wind gust for the date at this climate site (previous record was 39 mph set in 2008). At 1:28 pm, the ASOS at the airport measured a gust of 45 mph. By 1:30 pm, the Alachua County Emergency Management team reported multiple trees down as well as power lines across the county including in the Gainesville city limits.  The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated so this event could be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent pref-frontal squall line crossed the local forecast area during the late afternoon across SE Georgia and continuing eastward exiting the NE Florida Atlantic coast through midnight.  There was very strong speed shear over the local area with strong 0-6km shear around 55 knots, 0-1km helicity over 300 m2/s2, and sufficient CAPE for severe weather. The limiting factor was fairly weak mid-level lapse rates. Widespread wind damage occurred with this line with gusts of 40-50 mph and severe strength bowing segments producing wind damage and gusts of 60-76 mph. A isolated tornado briefly touched down in Waycross.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down across Suwannee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level flow on 6/13 and 6/14 caused our typically seabreeze thunderstorms activity to stay almost stationary when they formed. This caused flooding street, stranded cars, and water entering apartments in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported along Daniels Pkwy in Lee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm along the seabreeze boundary produced a quick lived tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down in Sarasota south of Hwy 72 and just east of I-75. Location based on photos and radar at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was in place (PWAT 2.5) with the area on the east side of TS |Cristobal as the storm approached the LA Gulf Coast. Waves of moderate to heavy rainfall with isolated embedded thunderstorms pivoted northward across the local forecast area into the evening with a more solid band of moderate to heavy rainfall developing across north Florida as low level convergence increased between a lifting frontal zones across south-central  FL and a persistent low level ridge axis holding firm just north of the Altamaha River basin. This ridge will limit the northward extend/expansion of the convective band into Sun, and generally focused a W-E oriented swath of heavy rainfall generally near and south of the I-10 corridor overnight into Sunday morning on 6/7.  The ridge strengthened north of the Altamaha Region Sun afternoon 6/7, which shunt the corridor of heavy rain SSW toward the I-75 corridor of NE FL with drier mid level air (700-500 mb) decreasing rain chances across SE GA and eastern NE FL into Sun evening. In addition to heavy rainfall potential, there was a low tornado threat for NE FL into the evening of 6/6 with elevated 0-1 km shear values 20-25 kts with low level ESE winds under increasing  SSW winds out of the GOMEX, with speeds increasing to 35-45 kts at 850 mb through 06z Sun 6/7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were damaged by thunderstorm wind gusts from Belleview to Summerfield along SE 95th Court, NW 20th Ave, 92th Loop and SE 126th Place.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 9 am, a trained spotter reported the roadway at Lenox Avenue near Lane and Normandy Blvd was inundated with flood water. At 905 am, the Duval County EM reported water up to 3 feet deep halfway up the stop sign at the intersection of McCoy Creek Blvd and King Street. At 914 am, local broadcast media reported that portions of Challen Ave, the Edgewood neighborhood, Cassat Ave, Normandy Blvd, Willowbranch Park and St. Johns Ave between Stockton and Publix were inundated with flood water. A spotter reported that cars were stalled in flood water along Edgewood Ave and Cassat Ave around 914 am. Fast moving water flowed across Cassat Ave near Normandy Ave and Cassat Ave near Edgewood Ave. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 330 pm a tornado touched down near Bradshaw Road where it damaged trees. The tornado tracked north-northeast and continued to snap trees as it tracked across SW Bellmont Drive and damage a small barn as it nearly paralleled State Road 47. The tornado path then tracked NNE along SW State Road 47 where it damaged large oak trees and power lines. The tornado peaked in intensity in this area with max winds around 85 mph. The tornado lifted just east of the State Road 47 along SW Wester Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was in place (PWAT 2.5) with the area on the east side of TS |Cristobal as the storm approached the LA Gulf Coast. Waves of moderate to heavy rainfall with isolated embedded thunderstorms pivoted northward across the local forecast area into the evening with a more solid band of moderate to heavy rainfall developing across north Florida as low level convergence increased between a lifting frontal zones across south-central  FL and a persistent low level ridge axis holding firm just north of the Altamaha River basin. This ridge will limit the northward extend/expansion of the convective band into Sun, and generally focused a W-E oriented swath of heavy rainfall generally near and south of the I-10 corridor overnight into Sunday morning on 6/7.  The ridge strengthened north of the Altamaha Region Sun afternoon 6/7, which shunt the corridor of heavy rain SSW toward the I-75 corridor of NE FL with drier mid level air (700-500 mb) decreasing rain chances across SE GA and eastern NE FL into Sun evening. In addition to heavy rainfall potential, there was a low tornado threat for NE FL into the evening of 6/6 with elevated 0-1 km shear values 20-25 kts with low level ESE winds under increasing  SSW winds out of the GOMEX, with speeds increasing to 35-45 kts at 850 mb through 06z Sun 6/7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site measured storm total daily rainfall of 5.38 inches along SE 110th Street in Belleview. Other observations in this area measured 4 to 5 inches of daily rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving frontal boundary brought deep tropical moisture across South Florida. The abundant moisture translated into heavy rainfall across the east coast metro, where flooding materialized across areas in Palm Beach and Broward that received repeated rounds of showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida highway patrol reported the ponding of water on some area highways in northern Broward County. A rain gauge near the intersection of the Sawgrass Expressway with the Florida Turnpike reported 2.21 inches of rain had fallen, with around three quarters of an inch of that falling in the last hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front aided in lofting a focused pool of moisture into South Florida. As the Gulf breeze developed, the abundant moisture combined with enhanced lifting to result in a few strong thunderstorms that produced flooding associated with heavy rainfall in Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos via Twitter show street flooding in Naples near 2nd ave North and 9th street North.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving frontal boundary brought deep tropical moisture across South Florida. The abundant moisture translated into heavy rainfall across the east coast metro, where flooding materialized across areas in Palm Beach and Broward that received repeated rounds of showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida highway patrol reported the ponding of water on some area highways in southern Palm Beach County. A nearby rain gauge at West Lakes reported 2.11 inches of rain had fallen, with around an inch of that falling in the last hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm became severe and produced wind damage near College Park in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed a report of large tree branches downed onto several cars at Albert Park near the intersection of Edgewater Drive and Vassar Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved between Grand Bahama and Southeast Florida in the morning of August 2. Isaias tracked north, moving nearly parallel to the eastern Florida coast over the next 24-36 hours, making its closest approach when the center was approximately 36 miles east-southeast of Fort Pierce Inlet. Outer rain bands on the western side of Isaias's circulation moved onshore of the east-central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds along the barrier islands, and gusts to tropical storm force west of the Intracoastal Waterway. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities. Tropical Storm Isaias produced minor beach erosion from elevated tide levels of approximately 1 foot above predicted tide values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Martin County, sustained tropical storm force winds affected the barrier islands, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county from early morning through late afternoon August 2. The highest sustained wind was measured at House of Refuge in Stuart (48 mph from the east at 0654LST on August 2) and the peak gust was also House of Refuge in Stuart (52 mph from the east at 0733LST on August 2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest steering flow focus the dominant west coast sea breeze to converge with the east coast sea breeze during the afternoon and early evening east of the Highway 301 corridor toward the I-95 corridor. Convective ingredients including cool 500 mb temps of -10 to -11, ML Cape of near 1000 J/kg and a weak mid level short wave traversing the area overhead during peak sea breeze collisions created a few severe storms which produced large hail over NE FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was reported at First Coast High School. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Ormond Beach man was pulled out of the surf after struggling in a rip current at Ormond Beach. Beach Safety personnel performed life-saving measures but the man was pronounced dead at the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 47-year-old Ormond Beach man was pulled out of the surf after struggling in a rip current. The man entered the water with a female companion in an unguarded area near the 1200 block of north Ormond Beach around 1500LST. Another person that came to the beach with the victim saw both the man and woman struggling in the water and called 9-1-1. Beach Safety personnel stated the man was unresponsive when they got him out of the water. Lifesaving measures were initiated but the man was pronounced dead at the hospital. The woman that was with him was assisted safely from the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two deadly rip current incidents were reported across the western Florida Panhandle on October 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 19 year old male drowned due to a rip current near Scenic Highway 98 in Destin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two deadly rip current incidents were reported across the western Florida Panhandle on October 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 49 year old male drowned due to a rip current approximately 5 miles east of Pensacola Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse surface trough axis was north of the Altamaha River basin with very high dew pts in the low/mid 70s across the entire forecast area south of the boundary. The 12z|JAX RAOB showed less deep layer moisture (PWAT 1.6 inches) and stronger SW steering flow across the entire area from 10-15 kts south of I-10 across NE FL to 20 kts across our SE GA zones. There was drier air in the low/mid levels where the steepest lapse rates were near 6.8 C/km between 790 mb and 617 mb roughly. SE GA 0-6 km bulk shear was 20-25 kts. Ingredients favored pulse strong to severe storms with gusty downburst winds the main hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was struck by lightning near Blanding Blvd and Doctors Inlet. The cost of damage was unknown but estimated for Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal heavy rainfall occurred as low pressure drifted northward offshore of the NE Florida Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of rainfall occurred near Watson Road near St. Augustine Shores in 2.5 hours. Minor flooding was reported on U.S. Highway 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal heavy rainfall occurred as low pressure drifted northward offshore of the NE Florida Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hr rainfall total of 5.5 inches was measured in Princess Place Estates. A couple of other gauges in the area measured 4.5 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze storms caused a lightning structure fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lighting caused a house fire in Mandarin along Grand Lakes Drive South. The time of the damage was based on NLDN data. The cost of damage was unknown but estimated for the inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry period with near critical relative humidity were near 30% with light SW winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida Forest Service reported a wildfire about 150 acres in the Juniper Wilderness of the Ocala NF. The time given was the approximate start time of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea  breeze thunderstorms developed over a moist and unstable airmass. Cool mid level temperatures with 500 mb around -10 degC and modestly unstable lapse rates with dry air in the hail growth zone supported pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Keystone Heights AWOS stationed measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of high tropical moisture with PWAT over 2 inches was across the local area with fairly light WSW steering flow around 10 kts. This regime pushed the west coast sea breeze inland where it merge with the river breeze and east coast sea breeze near the St. Johns River basin and produced a few strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was damaged at 1028 County Road 13 South. The time of damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three people working for Red Stag Contracting were struck by a lightning strike along Iris Street in Middleburg while working on a roof. All victims were transported to Orange Park Medical Center. One victim, a 35 year old male, was struck directly and went into cardiac arrest. He was revived via CPR on scene and was later transferred to the UF Health Shands Hospital in Gainesville, FL. He remained in a coma until his passing on 6/9/2020, at 15:45 EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along outflow boundary and sea breeze collisions. These storms produced two tornadoes in western Volusia County, including a rare August EF-2. In addition, other thunderstorms produced strong winds and hail along the Volusia and Brevard County coasts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Damage Survey confirmed that a strong tornado moved through North DeLand along a 4.6 mile long path with peak winds estimated between 110-120 mph. The tornado began around the intersection of W Minnesota Ave and N Ridgewood Ave, near the West Minnesota Estates neighborhood. The tornado generally tracked east-northeast across the city resulting in many downed medium to large tree branches, downed power lines, and numerous large trees were uprooted, which led to many local and major roadways being blocked. Many homes and vehicles suffered moderate to major damage as large tree branches or entire trees fell on them.||The tornado briefly intensified just southwest of the intersection of W Plymouth Avenue and N Spring Garden Avenue where EF-1 damage occurred. Two storage facility buildings suffered major roof collapse with winds estimated at 80-90 mph. The tornado continued to produce EF-0 damage along and south of W Plymouth Avenue as it moved towards Woodland Blvd. ||As the tornado reached Woodland Blvd. it quickly intensified and reached its widest point at approximately 550 yards. Extensive EF-1 damage was seen from Woodland Blvd. east to Amelia Avenue, and from E Kentucky Ave south to E Woodmont Road, with winds estimated between 90-100 mph. Damage was most significant along E Washington Ave between N Alabama Ave and N Amelia Ave, just east of US Hwy 17 where winds are estimated between 110-120 mph. Two homes had total roof loss, with a few others receiving moderate to major roof and structural damage. Several healthy tree trunks were found snapped and twisted near the base, with numerous smaller trees snapped midway. Videos taken along N Woodland Blvd revealed that a box delivery truck was tipped over and a car was flipped.||The tornado dissipated just east of the Lake Lindley subdivision between Jacobs Road and Lake Talmadge Drive where many large branches were snapped near the tops of the trees. ||There was one injury and no fatalities reported. Preliminary damage estimates from the Volusia County Property Appraiser indicate total damage of approximately $8,010,339. There were 163 damaged structures, with 49 sustaining minor damage, 40 sustaining major damage, and 5 that were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along outflow boundary and sea breeze collisions. These storms produced two tornadoes in western Volusia County, including a rare August EF-2. In addition, other thunderstorms produced strong winds and hail along the Volusia and Brevard County coasts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Damage Survey confirmed that a brief EF-0 tornado impacted the Sunset Hills subdivision east of Orange City. The tornado tracked along a 0.1 mile long path, with maximum winds estimated between 65-75 mph. The tornado had a maximum width of approximately 70 yards. Several large tree branches were downed on both sides of County Road 4101, between Sunnyside Ave and E Graves Ave. A mobile home was destroyed after its roof caved in at the intersection of Sunnyside Ave and County Road 4101. Preliminary damage estimates from the Volusia County Property Appraiser was $40,000, with one structure destroyed. There were no reported injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving and training thunderstorms along a cold front resulted in flash flooding across part of the western Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding was reported along Gulf Breeze Parkway in Gulf Breeze. A port of Da Lisa Road and the intersection of Garden Road and Commerce Road were closed near Bagdad. Flooding was also reported near the intersection of Raughton Road and Clearview Street near Garcon Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon sea breeze collisions resulted in a severe thunderstorm that moved over Orlando International Airport in Orange County, and produced a strong wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Orlando International Airport, KMCO, measured a peak wind gust of 55 knots or 63 mph from the southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Mexico helped to pump moist and unstable air across South Florida during the evening and nighttime hours of January 31st, and early morning of February 1st. Strong thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front over the eastern Gulf of Mexico, and moved east across the southern Florida peninsula. These storms spawned a tornado in the Everglades of Southwest Florida, as well as gusty winds and heavy rainfall across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The forward-propagating component of a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) advanced onshore along the Southwest Florida coast during the late-evening hours of Friday, January 31. Embedded mesovortices intermittently strengthened while |this activity moved onshore from the Gulf waters. ||One of the stronger and tighter circulations over Mainland Monroe County of South|Florida was accompanied by a well-defined Tornadic Debris Signature (TDS). This signature was characterized by a relative minimum in correlation coefficient per KAMX radar data, and corresponded with an embedded inflection in the zone of enhanced |reflectivity accompanying the QLCS that also aligned with a well-defined mesovortex. This TDS occurred over the Everglades, and was used as the foundation for this tornado report. ||The lack of conclusively surveyed damage indicators precludes the delineation |of an exact damage rating. As a result, an unknown rating has been assigned to this tornado. However, the application of established research results on the aforementioned radar data -- which provides statistical estimates of tornado probabilities and wind |speeds based on WSR-88D data and environmental information -- suggests that this tornado could have been a high-end EF0, with peak wind speeds around 80 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at 83 Institution Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal line of strong to severe storms moved across NE FL and SE GA during the afternoon and pressed offshore of the Atlantic coast into the evening.  Surface based CAPEs rose up to 1000 J/kg over parts of the Suwannee Valley and inland SE GA. This instability shifted east across the remainder of NE FL into the evening coinciding with elevated moisture and dynamics that produced a few severe storms and also 2 confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Royal Palms and Belle Terre Parkway. The cost of damage was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust devil was spotted in Fort Myers on a warm and mostly clear day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media shared a viewer video taken from a building near the intersection of Bass and Summerlin Roads looking north, of what appears to be a dust devil. The video was taken on a warm and dry day, with mostly clear skies, showing a tall funnel rotating for about a minute. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying frontal boundary across South Florida became the focus for convective initiation. Strong thunderstorms developed over the Gulf of Mexico before pushing across the peninsula and then offshore into the Atlantic. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 64 mph/ 56 knots was recorded by Wxflow station XMGN in  Mangonia Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph / 53 knots was recorded by the Weatherbug site WTPBH located at West Palm Beach Fire Station 1.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports received from the public and broadcast media of quarter size hail in The Acreage. Small dents to the roofs of vehicles was observed, as well as small branches broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry March and beginning of April led to severe drought conditions which developed over interior and western sections of southern Florida by the 3rd week of April. Several wildfires were reported in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Naples Municipal Airport recorded only a trace of rainfall for the entire month of March, and only 0.81 inches through the first 3 weeks of April. This led to the development of severe drought conditions across all of Collier County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry March and beginning of April led to severe drought conditions which developed over interior and western sections of southern Florida by the 3rd week of April. Several wildfires were reported in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts for all of March and the first 3 weeks of April were generally in the 1 inch range across most of inland Miami-Dade County. As a result, water levels in the Everglades reached near-record low levels. This led to the development of severe drought conditions across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening surface low pressure area moved across the Tri-State region.  A warm front lifted northward through the area, and a cold front moved through during the afternoon hours. Scattered thunderstorms moved across the area, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial damage was noted in far eastern Jefferson County along HWY 257 near its intersection with Walker Spring Rd. Damage consisting of many hundreds of snapped and uprooted trees continued east northeast into Madison County eventually crossing HWY 19. There was also at least one wooden power pole snapped.  This tornado was rated EF1 with max winds estimated at 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening surface low pressure area moved across the Tri-State region.  A warm front lifted northward through the area, and a cold front moved through during the afternoon hours. Scattered thunderstorms moved across the area, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial damage, consisting of a few dozen trees uprooted and snapped, was noted on a farm on the west side of HWY 221 just south of I-10. Damage, consisting of hundreds of snapped and uprooted trees, continued to the east northeast crossing Sundown Creek Rd. and eventually I-10. After the tornado crossed I-10 it moved into inaccessible areas of the Hixton Swamp Conservation Area. Based on radar the tornado remained on the ground continuously until damage became accessible again along HWY 360A just south of the intersection with CR 35. Damage to several trees at this location was the last damage evident from this tornado. Damage further northeast in Madison was from a separate brief spinup after this tornado lifted.  This tornado was rated EF1 with max winds estimated at 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening surface low pressure area moved across the Tri-State region.  A warm front lifted northward through the area, and a cold front moved through during the afternoon hours. Scattered thunderstorms moved across the area, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A business suffered roof uplift and had a window blown in. There was significant water damage inside the business.  There was an outbuilding damaged at the south Duval Street location as well.  There was also scattered minor tree damage in the area. This tornado was rated EF0 with max winds estimated at 75 mph.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal line of strong to severe storms moved across NE FL and SE GA during the afternoon and pressed offshore of the Atlantic coast into the evening.  Surface based CAPEs rose up to 1000 J/kg over parts of the Suwannee Valley and inland SE GA. This instability shifted east across the remainder of NE FL into the evening coinciding with elevated moisture and dynamics that produced a few severe storms and also 2 confirmed tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with peak winds near 85 mph briefly touched down north of County Road 252 south of Live Oak. The tornado damaged a chicken house and trees in the area. One large tree branch fell on a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions which began in March continued through the first half of May, leading to an expansion of severe drought conditions from SW Florida eastward to most of southern Miami-Dade County. Several large wildfires flared up across the Everglades and Big Cypress, most notably in Golden Gate Estates and near the borders of Miami-Dade, Collier, and Mainland Monroe counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than three-quarters of an inch of rain fell during the first half of May across southern portions of Miami-Dade County, leading to the development of severe drought conditions. Surface and underground water levels dropped to around 10% of normal in some areas. Rainfall increased significantly during the second half of May, putting an end to the severe drought conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than three-quarters of an inch of rain fell during the first half of May across inland portions of Miami-Dade County, leading to the continuation of severe drought conditions. Surface and underground water levels dropped to around 10% of normal in some areas. Rainfall increased significantly during the second half of May, putting an end to the severe drought conditions by the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than a quarter of an inch of rain fell during the first half of May across most of Collier County, leading to the continuation of severe drought conditions. Surface and underground water levels dropped to around 10% of normal in some areas, and burn bans were in effect. Rainfall increased significantly during the second half of May, putting an end to the severe drought conditions by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rex block characterized by a deep upper-level low across the Tennessee Valley Region and an upper-level high across the Great Lakes Region gave way to an upper-level cold pocket across the southeastern portions of the United states. Simultaneously, a mid-level vorticity lobe rotated across the region which also aided in an increase of convection, especially due to the combination of lingering boundaries and sea breeze formation. Forecast soundings also showed an inverted V as well as favorable DCAPE values, which were supportive for a few damaging wind gusts with the taller, more robust, updrafts contained hail. Furthermore, tornado potential increased with favorable low level winds and shear across land. Finally, with an overall weaker flow aloft and PWATs approaching the 2 inch mark, some localized flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video via social media reported nickel size hail near Tamarac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 41-year-old man was killed in Pt. Saint Lucie after being struck by lightning at a construction site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Lucie County Emergency Management reported that a 41-year-old man was killed after being struck by lightning at an outdoor construction site in Tradition, near Port Saint Lucie. The man was struck by lightning at approximately 1450LST and was quickly discovered by his coworkers to be unconscious. A coworker called 911 and emergency services transported the injured man to a local hospital where he later died from his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along the Martin County coast, and trained northward over the same area, resulting in persistent heavy rainfall and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous training thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall in coastal Martin County that resulted in 6-9 inches of rain, with isolated higher totals of 12-14 inches. The heavy rain fell in a 15-18 hour period from around sunrise through the late evening. The localized flooding and associated impacts all occurred in the 5-6 hour period from late afternoon into the evening after hours of continuous heavy rainfall. Stuart Police Department reported that Commerce Avenue was temporarily closed due to high water from Market Place south into Port Salerno. Martin County Fire Rescue reported flooding near the intersection of Mulberry Drive and Linda Road. Trained spotters in Hobe Sound reported numerous local roads were flooded with cars having difficulty passing through the standing water. FAA Control Tower personnel at the Stuart Witham Field Airport reported 2-3 inches of standing water on the runway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions across South Florida allowed a wildfire to burn in Collier, Mainland-Monroe, and Miami-Dade counties for several weeks. The fire quickly spread over county lines and lingered due to lack of rain and gusty winds. Smoke produced very low to no visibility in the area and along nearby roadways. The fire caused the loss of one private structure and two out buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of several road closures, including US-41, due to wildfire and smoke from Big Cypress National Preserve. The fire has caused US-41 to be closed between SR-29 in Collier County and Krome Ave in Miami. The fire caused the loss of one private structure and two out buildings despite successful burnout operations and firefighters continued efforts to clear around as many structures as possible. The source of the fire was human caused, a truck fire. The total acreage of the fire was 25,834 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions across South Florida allowed a wildfire to burn in Collier County for several weeks (fire ignited in April). The fire quickly spread and lingered due to lack of rain and gusty winds. Smoke produced very low to no visibility in the area and along the interstate to the north periodically over the course of the fire's lifespan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Park Service in Big Cypress National Preserve reported a wildfire that began in April and became 100% contained in May. The wildfire was in the northeast|corner of the preserve and was located 5 miles north of I-75 at mile marker 55 just west of the L-28 Interceptor Canal. Major Interstate I-75 was closed periodically throughout the time of the wildfire due to heavy smoke and to allow fire fighting officials/vehicles quicker access to the fire. There were no reported injuries or evacuations other than road closures. The size of the wildfire, due to better mapping data collection, ended up being approximately 611 acres. The cause of the wildfire, not a prescribed fire, is still under investigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions across South Florida allowed two wildfires to burn before combining into one wildfire across Collier County. Wildfire lasted for about a week and a half. The fire quickly spread and lingered due to lack of rain and gusty winds. Smoke produced very low to no visibility in the area and along nearby roadways. The fire caused the loss of structures, warranted mandatory evacuations, and closed roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Smoke produced very low to no visibility in the area and along nearby roadways, prompting the closure of major interstate I-75 between MM 80 and 101 periodically over the lifespan of the fire. Twelve residences have reportedly been destroyed and thirty three outbuildings were destroyed. The NWS Miami NOAA Weather Radio Transmitter phone lines, located in Collier County, also sustained damage with the lines burning through. A mandatory evacuation order has been issued for approximately 2,000 residents in the area. Total approximate acreage burned was 8,250 acres. Cause of fire still unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a thunderstorms caused roof damage to a home on Gulf Shore Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level troughing remained prominent across the Gulf of Mexico with a speed max extending east-northeast from the Bay of Campeche into the northwest Caribbean Sea. A decaying Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) was lifting north through the Atlantic waters while surface troughing extended from the southern Florida Peninsula southwest into the southern Gulf of Mexico. Given the diffluent pattern aloft, subtle vorticity perturbations, the remnant MCV, and the surface trough, plentiful sources for lift were evident across South Florida that allowed for the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Additionally, plenty of moisture was distributed areawide with rainfall rates exceeding 2 inches per hour. Abundant moisture along with preceding heavy rainfall and antecedent conditions led to widespread flooding across Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Furthermore, increasing low-level shear behind the MCV whipped up a brief tornado in Miami-Dade County and a funnel cloud in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey team found damage consistent with an EF-1 tornado that developed around the Miami Everglades RV Resort and moved discontinuously northwest to near SW 197th Avenue and SW 156th Street. Several campers were tipped over at the Miami Everglades RV Resort, consistent with stronger winds around 90 mph per radar-based statistical model. The majority of the remaining damage was limited to trees and fencing starting east of Krome Avenue around SW 192nd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical disturbance over the South Florida Atlantic waters of Broward and Miami-Dade counties was forecast by NHC to continue moving northeastward into the Western Atlantic waters. NHC showed a 70 percent chance of development into a tropical system in the next 48 hours, mainly in the Northwest Bahamas or the Western Atlantic waters. As the disturbance moved northeastward, deeper moisture worked into the region from the south leading to the continuation of numerous showers and thunderstorms over eastern areas. As this convection progressed towards the east coast metro areas, strong wind gusts along with heavy rainfall and repeated rounds of convection lead to widespread flooding and standing water that lasted into the overnight hours of May 15 into May 16, well after precipitation ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS Employee reported extensive street flooding due to heavy rainfall in areas of Broward County, specifically Pompano Beach area. Water several inches deep and flowing near the intersection of Atlantic Blvd and US-1, the intersection of 15th St and North Andrews Ave, the intersection of 13th St and North Andrews Ave, and the intersection of North Andrews Ave and Copans Rd was reported. Some roadways/intersections were impassable. Multiple pictures received showing the excessive water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Arthur, which was moving over the outer banks of North Carolina, was forecast to continue moving northeastward off the Mid-Atlantic coastline. At the same time, the tail of Arthur, basically a trough of low pressure, extended into the southeastern portion of the country. This also combined with a mid to upper level area of low pressure moving into the Tennessee Valley and a trough of low pressure moving into the eastern Gulf of Mexico which allowed for a south southwesterly flow across South Florida. This allowed for the Gulf coast sea breeze to push inland as the east coast sea breeze remained pushed up against the east coast. This eventually lead to the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across all of South Florida. The primary impacts with these storms were heavy rainfall and associated flooding, frequent lightning strikes, small hail, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of rain, with measured amounts ranging from 4 to 8 inches, and locally higher amounts, caused flooding in portions of Boynton and Delray Beach. Broadcast media shared a video that showed flooding near the intersection of Congress Ave and Woolbright Rd in Boynton Beach. One car stalled while others turned around. The Weather Underground station KFLDELRA49 in the Sabal Lakes Community of Delray Beach measured 5.41 inches of rain whereas the Weather Underground station KFLDELRA71 near Lake Ida in Delray Beach measured 7.37 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that dumped over a foot of rain over portions of the east coast the past few days is now Tropical Storm Bertha, which made landfall along the South Carolina coast. A series of weak upper-level impulses also slid across South Florida throughout the course of the day. Consequently, wind flow (and the steering flow) continued to have a slight southwesterly component which pushed showers and thunderstorms towards the east coast. Some drying aloft and cooling 500 mb temps allowed for some steeping of mid-level lapse rates. Plentiful low-level moisture was also still available with PWATs close to 1.6 inches. This abundance of moisture allowed convection to tap into it, creating widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours with high rainfall rates. Unfortunately, this was the area that continued to be the most susceptible to hydrological issues with a continued Flood Watch through 8 PM EDT. Lastly, given sufficient precipitation loading and DCAPE over 1000J/kg, a few stronger wind gusts occurred along with some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter inch hail was reported with a strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 62-year-old man from Orlando was pulled from the water after being caught in a rip current, but later died at the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 1041LST, a 62-year-old man and his wife, both from Orlando, entered the surf at an unguarded beach near the 4600 block of South Atlantic Avenue just north of Ponce Inlet. The couple was quickly overtaken by a rip current and rough surf conditions. A passerby noticed the woman calling for help and called 911. Volusia County Ocean Rescue and lifeguards pulled the woman, unharmed, from the surf. The man was pulled from the surf unresponsive and unconscious. First responders administered CPR, reviving the man, then he was taken to Halifax Health Medical Center. However, he later died from his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rex block characterized by a deep upper-level low across the Tennessee Valley Region and an upper-level high across the Great Lakes Region gave way to an upper-level cold pocket across the southeastern portions of the United states. Simultaneously, a mid-level vorticity lobe rotated across the region which also aided in an increase of convection, especially due to the combination of lingering boundaries and sea breeze formation. Forecast soundings also showed an inverted V as well as favorable DCAPE values, which were supportive for a few damaging wind gusts with the taller, more robust, updrafts contained hail. Furthermore, tornado potential increased with favorable low level winds and shear across land. Finally, with an overall weaker flow aloft and PWATs approaching the 2 inch mark, some localized flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video via social media reported quarter size hail near Sample Road and the Sawgrass Expressway near Coral Springs.

Simultaneously, a mid-level vorticity lobe rotated across the region which also aided in an increase of convection, especially due to the combination of lingering boundaries and sea breeze formation. Forecast soundings also showed an inverted V as well as favorable DCAPE values, which were supportive for a few damaging wind gusts with the taller, more robust, updrafts contained hail. Furthermore, tornado potential increased with favorable low level winds and shear across land. Finally, with an overall weaker flow aloft and PWATs approaching the 2 inch mark, some localized flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter driving on the Florida Turnpike near US 27 reported hail up to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level troughing remained prominent across the Gulf of Mexico with a speed max extending east-northeast from the Bay of Campeche into the northwest Caribbean Sea. A decaying Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) was lifting north through the Atlantic waters while surface troughing extended from the southern Florida Peninsula southwest into the southern Gulf of Mexico. Given the diffluent pattern aloft, subtle vorticity perturbations, the remnant MCV, and the surface trough, plentiful sources for lift were evident across South Florida that allowed for the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Additionally, plenty of moisture was distributed areawide with rainfall rates exceeding 2 inches per hour. Abundant moisture along with preceding heavy rainfall and antecedent conditions led to widespread flooding across Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Furthermore, increasing low-level shear behind the MCV whipped up a brief tornado in Miami-Dade County and a funnel cloud in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS employee reported flooding impacting vehicle travel and entering structures near Alton Road and 13th Street in Miami Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions which began in March continued through the first half of May, leading to an expansion of severe drought conditions from SW Florida eastward to most of southern Miami-Dade County. Several large wildfires flared up across the Everglades and Big Cypress, most notably in Golden Gate Estates and near the borders of Miami-Dade, Collier, and Mainland Monroe counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than a half-inch of rain fell during the first half of May across most of Hendry County, leading to the continuation of severe drought conditions. Burn bans were in effect for most of the month. Rainfall increased significantly during the second half of May, putting an end to the severe drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-dawn severe thunderstorms developed across SE GA on the northern side of a lifting warm front across N Florida. Elevated instability and strong dynamics coincided over SE GA ahead of a surface low that track eastward from the FL panhandle toward the local forecast area through the morning hours. Through the late morning hours, the shortwave trough aloft lifted northeast toward the mid-Atlantic coastline into the afternoon. This pulled a very strong low level jet of 50-70 kts NE as well, but continued to produce a favorable environment for severe storms through midday as the warm front lifted north across SE GA placing NE FL in the warm sector under strong dynamics for severe storm potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A construction trailer was blown across Interstate 75 at the Ocala Weigh Station near Belleview. The time of damage was based on radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Melbourne staff conducted a damage survey and found no damage along the radar-estimated rotational track of a supercell thunderstorm, which moved from Seminole County to the Brevard County coast. The survey team searched along a path from Heathrow to Scottsmoor. However, based on numerous videos received from trained spotters and the general public, NWS Melbourne confirms that a waterspout developed on Lake Jesup just east of State Road 417. The waterspout continued east for approximately 2.75 miles, and came onshore briefly as an EF-0 tornado (peak winds estimated 60-65 mph) within an unpopulated area adjacent to the lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line with embedded supercells will moved across interior southeast Georgia during the early morning hours and reached the Suwannee Valley just before daybreak.  Very strong shear and instability phased across SE GA during the morning hours where there were supercells, one that produced two tornado touchdowns. The pre-frontal line slowed down and gradually weakened as it moved into NE Florida into the afternoon, but severe storms continued to be possible due to weakening dynamics and remaining surface based instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree and power line wind damage occurred across the county from thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved slowly across SE GA and the Suwannee Valley during the ealry morning hours. Mid and upper level shortwave energy from the Gulf of Mexico coincided aloft near the front which produced strong to severe storms. The storm prediction center had a Marginal risk of Severe thunderstorms over the region and 40-50 knots of 0-6 Km Bulk Shear along with 500-1000 J/KG of MLCAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in the Biscayne Downs Subdivision near the Broward River, just west of I-95. The tornado knocked down trees along Sarasota Lane and Secretariat Drive. One tree was snapped an fell on a home along Sarasota Lane. It destroyed a sunroom along Riva Ridge Drive. The tornado likely lifted before it crossed the Broward River with sporadic wind damage occurring through Oceanway before touching back down along Grover Road, where significant tree damage occurred. Tree damage occurred along Boney Road and Starratt Road near Edwards Creek including a snapped tree that fell on a truck with other uprooted trees in the area. Local broadcast media relayed a video of the tornado as it bypassed Black Hammock Island near the A1A and Nassau Sound bridge next to Amelia Island State Park. Tornado damage was not continuous along the path. The path width was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moving across central Florida produced two tornadoes, one in Okahumpka in Lake County, then another in DeLand in Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service staff conducted a damage survey and confirmed an EF-0 tornado occurred in Okahumpka on January 4, 2020. Peak winds were estimated between 65 and 75 mph, along a discontinuous path of 2.38 miles, with a maximum width of 175 yards. The tornado generally moved northeast beginning just west of the Florida Turnpike near mile marker 295, crossing mostly wooded areas, Debbie Road, and the western side of the Holiday RV Village before dissipating. Damage estimates indicate significant roof damage to 3 to 5 mobile homes, one large oak tree was toppled, and numerous large branches of weak/old trees were downed. One resident received minor injuries when a tree branch came through the roof of his manufactured home at the RV Village. The individual self-transported to the hospital for treatment of a minor cut to his head.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moving across central Florida produced two tornadoes, one in Okahumpka in Lake County, then another in DeLand in Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service staff conducted a damage survey and confirmed a EF-1 tornado in occurred in DeLand on January 4, 2020. Peak winds were estimated between 90 and 100 mph, along a 4.39 mile long path, with a maximum width of 265 yards. A DeLand resident on a large paddleboat on Lake Beresford observed the waterspout/tornado touching down just southwest of his location on the lake. The tornado moved onshore causing tree damage to a nearby property near South Beresford Road. The tornado continued generally northeast clipping the southern side of Woodward Avenue Elementary School, crossing multiple streets, subdivisions, a large park, a nursing home, and dissipated near the intersection of Colorado Avenue and New York Avenue. Damage estimates indicate several large trees toppled, numerous large branches downed, two homes experienced major roof damage, significant roof damage to the Veterans of Foreign Wars (VFW) building, structural damage at Melching Field and Earl Brown Park, 8-10 power poles snapped at the base, and 10 additional structures received lesser, minor-moderate damage. City of DeLand officials estimate the damage in the city to be around $25,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over loop current in the Gulf of Mexico ahead of an approaching cold front. As the line progressed eastward, strong daytime heating allowed for an Atlantic Sea breeze to develop across, which resulted several rounds of thunderstorms that produced damaging wind gusts and hail across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patio-area tall metal enclosure, associated covering, and ancillary items toppled and damaged in association with a cluster of severe thunderstorms that moved through the Miami area yesterday. Location is near intersection of NW 64th street and NW 18th |Avenue in Liberty City. Report received via social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reported a large tree limb snapped and resting over adjacent fence in Hialeah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical disturbance in the Florida Straits was forecast by NHC to move northeastward into the Atlantic waters of South Florida before continuing to move northeastward into the Western Atlantic waters. NHC showed a 70 percent chance of development into a tropical system in the next 48 hours, mainly in the Northwest Bahamas or the Western Atlantic waters. As the disturbance moved northeastward, deeper moisture worked into the region from the south leading to numerous showers and thunderstorms over eastern areas. As this convection progressed towards the east coast metro areas, especially Broward and Miami-Dade counties, heavy rainfall and repeated rounds of convection lead to widespread flooding and standing water that lasted into the overnight hours of May 15 into May 16, well after precipitation ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain, with reduced visibility, began in the morning hours and caused a major accident involving a tractor-trailer on the Florida's Turnpike in South Miami-Dade County, near SW 137th Avenue, which closed down most lanes to southbound traffic. Injuries/fatalities unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical disturbance over the South Florida Atlantic waters of Broward and Miami-Dade counties was forecast by NHC to continue moving northeastward into the Western Atlantic waters. NHC showed a 70 percent chance of development into a tropical system in the next 48 hours, mainly in the Northwest Bahamas or the Western Atlantic waters. As the disturbance moved northeastward, deeper moisture worked into the region from the south leading to the continuation of numerous showers and thunderstorms over eastern areas. As this convection progressed towards the east coast metro areas, strong wind gusts along with heavy rainfall and repeated rounds of convection lead to widespread flooding and standing water that lasted into the overnight hours of May 15 into May 16, well after precipitation ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful downburst produced a wind gust of 58 mph (50 kt) which was measured by the Sunrise Weatherstem Mesonet (1343W). Site is elevated 6 feet above ground level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Arthur was forecast by the NHC to continue moving northeastward towards the outer banks of North Carolina while a mid-upper level low over TX moves eastward. This allowed for the wind flow over South FL to be SSW which allowed the Gulf Sea Breeze to form along with the Atlantic Sea Breeze. Plenty of residual tropical moisture across the area, along with the eventual sea breeze collision, lead to scattered to numerous strong showers and thunderstorms that eventually pushed eastward towards the east coast metro areas (thanks to the SSW flow). Heavy rain and flooding along with strong wind gusts and small hail were reported. Enhanced helicity/shear in Palm Beach County even led to the development of a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS Employee reported scattered small to medium sized downed tree branches in Parkland, near the Parkland Farmers Market and along the intersection of Parkside Dr and Holmberg Rd. Wind gust estimated based off radar at approximately 40 mph (35 kt), but were likely higher on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest steering flow pushed a stronger west coast sea breeze inland across NE FL where a moist and unstable airmass fueled scattered strong to pulse severe storms in the late afternoon and evening as storms approached the east coast and interacted with the sea breeze and boundary mergers. Additional storms developed across southeast Georgia in the afternoon and early evening ahead of a mid level short wave trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on a home and car along the 200 block of Edison Drive in Meadowbrook Terrace.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS Employee reported downed tree limbs (green palm fronds and oak limbs of 1-2 inches in diameter with a few 3 inches in diameter) in Weston, near Savanna Trail and Saddle Club Rd. Cypress Bay High School (closest Mesonet) measured a wind gust of  47 mph (41 kt).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Arthur, which was moving over the outer banks of North Carolina, was forecast to continue moving northeastward off the Mid-Atlantic coastline. At the same time, the tail of Arthur, basically a trough of low pressure, extended into the southeastern portion of the country. This also combined with a mid to upper level area of low pressure moving into the Tennessee Valley and a trough of low pressure moving into the eastern Gulf of Mexico which allowed for a south southwesterly flow across South Florida. This allowed for the Gulf coast sea breeze to push inland as the east coast sea breeze remained pushed up against the east coast. This eventually lead to the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across all of South Florida. The primary impacts with these storms were heavy rainfall and associated flooding, frequent lightning strikes, small hail, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel sized hail and wind gusts high enough to knock down some weak tree branches at Big Blue Trace and Wellington Trace in Wellington, FL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough located across the Gulf of Mexico lifted north and dampened as weak high pressure gradually encompassed the region. Low-level troughing across central Florida gradually shifted away to the north as Atlantic ridging nosed in from the east. This allowed for southerly low-level winds to prevail. An abundance of moisture created widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours with high rainfall rates.The greatest shower and thunderstorm coverage occurred over the east coast metro areas, with a focus over Miami-Dade County. Unfortunately, this was the area most susceptible to hydrological issues given 48 hour rain totals in the 3 to 9 inch range with isolated higher amounts. Flooding was significant and very slow to recede, lasting up to 48 hours in some parts of northern Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various reports from members of the public. Cars stalling out at the intersection of NW 87th Ave and NW 33rd St. Also, NW 84th Ave flooded between NW 27th St and NW 33rd St. Kids Corner Child Development center reported parking lot flooded and water approaching the building. Public called in and said they are stranded at their business just NE of Miami International Airport due to flooding and impassable streets. Public reported water causing roadways to be nearly impassable and nearing structures near the intersection of NW 87th Ave and NW 25th St in Doral. Public reported that the off ramp from the Airport Expressway to US-27 northbound was flooded with water up to 4 feet. Many cars stranded in the area due to flooding. Public reported ongoing flooding on NW 87th Ave between NW 36th St and Highway 836. Damage assessments indicated at least minor flooding in the Medley and Doral warehouse districts. ||The ASOS at Miami International Airport measured 6.42 inches of rain in just two hours, indicative of the extremely high rainfall rates in the affected areas.||Flood waters were very slow to recede, with some areas with standing water and water inside structures for up to 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rex block characterized by a deep upper-level low across the Tennessee Valley Region and an upper-level high across the Great Lakes Region gave way to an upper-level cold pocket across the southeastern portions of the United states. Simultaneously, a mid-level vorticity lobe rotated across the region which also aided in an increase of convection, especially due to the combination of lingering boundaries and sea breeze formation. Forecast soundings also showed an inverted V as well as favorable DCAPE values, which were supportive for a few damaging wind gusts with the taller, more robust, updrafts contained hail. Furthermore, tornado potential increased with favorable low level winds and shear across land. Finally, with an overall weaker flow aloft and PWATs approaching the 2 inch mark, some localized flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo of a funnel cloud was reported in Boca Raton, west of I-95 and Spanish River Blvd, looking west toward Military Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that dumped over a foot of rain over portions of the east coast the past few days is now Tropical Storm Bertha, which made landfall along the South Carolina coast. A series of weak upper-level impulses also slid across South Florida throughout the course of the day. Consequently, wind flow (and the steering flow) continued to have a slight southwesterly component which pushed showers and thunderstorms towards the east coast. Some drying aloft and cooling 500 mb temps allowed for some steeping of mid-level lapse rates. Plentiful low-level moisture was also still available with PWATs close to 1.6 inches. This abundance of moisture allowed convection to tap into it, creating widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours with high rainfall rates. Unfortunately, this was the area that continued to be the most susceptible to hydrological issues with a continued Flood Watch through 8 PM EDT. Lastly, given sufficient precipitation loading and DCAPE over 1000J/kg, a few stronger wind gusts occurred along with some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down, or a large portion of a tree down, on the road in the vicinity of the intersection of A1A and Toney Penna Drive, requiring maintenance intervention for cleaning. This wind damage was in association with a cluster of thunderstorms that crossed portions of Palm Beach County during the afternoon hours. Estimated wind gust in the 50 to 60 kt (60-65 mph) range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was in place (PWAT 2.5) with the area on the east side of TS |Cristobal as the storm approached the LA Gulf Coast. Waves of moderate to heavy rainfall with isolated embedded thunderstorms pivoted northward across the local forecast area into the evening with a more solid band of moderate to heavy rainfall developing across north Florida as low level convergence increased between a lifting frontal zones across south-central  FL and a persistent low level ridge axis holding firm just north of the Altamaha River basin. This ridge will limit the northward extend/expansion of the convective band into Sun, and generally focused a W-E oriented swath of heavy rainfall generally near and south of the I-10 corridor overnight into Sunday morning on 6/7.  The ridge strengthened north of the Altamaha Region Sun afternoon 6/7, which shunt the corridor of heavy rain SSW toward the I-75 corridor of NE FL with drier mid level air (700-500 mb) decreasing rain chances across SE GA and eastern NE FL into Sun evening. In addition to heavy rainfall potential, there was a low tornado threat for NE FL into the evening of 6/6 with elevated 0-1 km shear values 20-25 kts with low level ESE winds under increasing  SSW winds out of the GOMEX, with speeds increasing to 35-45 kts at 850 mb through 06z Sun 6/7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station AV553 measured 6.51 inches of rain since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was across the eastern U.S. with a shortwave impulse rotating through the base of the trough across southeast Georgia in the afternoon which support strong to severe storms that developed along sea breeze and outflow boundary storms. The airmass was relatively dry for the Jacksonville area with a precipitable water value of 1.4 inches. Deeper moisture was confined to north central Florida and southward. Shear values included 0-6km shear around 25-30 knots with 500mb temperatures cooling than normal around -9C with dry air aloft. These ingredients favored damaging wind potential in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos were relayed of severe storm wind damage at 2880 NE 63rd Street, Ocala, FL 34479. Widespread trees were snapped and uprooted, some that landed on a pool covering.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Arthur was forecast by the NHC to continue moving northeastward towards the outer banks of North Carolina while a mid-upper level low over TX moves eastward. This allowed for the wind flow over South FL to be SSW which allowed the Gulf Sea Breeze to form along with the Atlantic Sea Breeze. Plenty of residual tropical moisture across the area, along with the eventual sea breeze collision, lead to scattered to numerous strong showers and thunderstorms that eventually pushed eastward towards the east coast metro areas (thanks to the SSW flow). Heavy rain and flooding along with strong wind gusts and small hail were reported. Enhanced helicity/shear in Palm Beach County even led to the development of a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS Employee reported multiple medium and large sized tree branches down near Hammock Oaks and Old Cutler Rd. Wind gust estimated based off radar at approximately 42 mph (36 kt), but were likely higher on the ground. Small hail (approximately 0.50 inches, estimated) was also reported with this thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Arthur, which was moving over the outer banks of North Carolina, was forecast to continue moving northeastward off the Mid-Atlantic coastline. At the same time, the tail of Arthur, basically a trough of low pressure, extended into the southeastern portion of the country. This also combined with a mid to upper level area of low pressure moving into the Tennessee Valley and a trough of low pressure moving into the eastern Gulf of Mexico which allowed for a south southwesterly flow across South Florida. This allowed for the Gulf coast sea breeze to push inland as the east coast sea breeze remained pushed up against the east coast. This eventually lead to the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across all of South Florida. The primary impacts with these storms were heavy rainfall and associated flooding, frequent lightning strikes, small hail, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS Employee reported significant flooding at the intersection of Mashta Drive and Allendale Road in Key Biscayne due to repeated rounds of showers and thunderstorms across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rex block characterized by a deep upper-level low across the Tennessee Valley Region and an upper-level high across the Great Lakes Region gave way to an upper-level cold pocket across the southeastern portions of the United states. Simultaneously, a mid-level vorticity lobe rotated across the region which also aided in an increase of convection, especially due to the combination of lingering boundaries and sea breeze formation. Forecast soundings also showed an inverted V as well as favorable DCAPE values, which were supportive for a few damaging wind gusts with the taller, more robust, updrafts contained hail. Furthermore, tornado potential increased with favorable low level winds and shear across land. Finally, with an overall weaker flow aloft and PWATs approaching the 2 inch mark, some localized flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported flooding of roadways and parking lots in eastern portions of Clewiston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low ejected northeastward and away from the southeast US which allowed for heights to rebound across South Florida as a ridge builds in from the western Atlantic. With the return of easterly flow, the Atlantic sea breeze pushed inland and across the interior while the Gulf Breeze remained pinned along the west coast. This allowed the main focus for afternoon convection to be in western portions of South Florida where the two sea breeze boundaries collided. With temperatures aloft rather cool along with steepened lapse rates, parcel accelerations were strong enough for the development of hail and strong downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures received via social media reported 3/4 inch hail near the intersection of Collier Blvd and Vanderbilt Beach Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions which began in March continued through the first half of May, leading to an expansion of severe drought conditions from SW Florida eastward to most of southern Miami-Dade County. Several large wildfires flared up across the Everglades and Big Cypress, most notably in Golden Gate Estates and near the borders of Miami-Dade, Collier, and Mainland Monroe counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than three-quarters of an inch of rain fell during the first half of May across southern portions of Coastal Miami-Dade County, mainly in the Homestead area, leading to the development of severe drought conditions. Surface and underground water levels dropped to around 10% of normal in some areas. Rainfall increased significantly during the second half of May, putting an end to the severe drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions which began in March continued through the first half of May, leading to an expansion of severe drought conditions from SW Florida eastward to most of southern Miami-Dade County. Several large wildfires flared up across the Everglades and Big Cypress, most notably in Golden Gate Estates and near the borders of Miami-Dade, Collier, and Mainland Monroe counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than a quarter of an inch of rain fell during the first half of May across most of Collier County, leading to the continuation of severe drought conditions. Surface and underground water levels dropped to around 10% of normal in some areas, and burn bans were in effect. Rainfall increased significantly during the second half of May, putting an end to the severe drought conditions by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry March and beginning of April led to severe drought conditions which developed over interior and western sections of southern Florida by the 3rd week of April. Several wildfires were reported in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts for all of March and the first 3 weeks of April were generally in the 1 inch range across all but the northeastern corner of Hendry County. As a result, water levels in the Everglades reached near-record low levels. This led to the development of severe drought conditions across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was in place (PWAT 2.5) with the area on the east side of TS |Cristobal as the storm approached the LA Gulf Coast. Waves of moderate to heavy rainfall with isolated embedded thunderstorms pivoted northward across the local forecast area into the evening with a more solid band of moderate to heavy rainfall developing across north Florida as low level convergence increased between a lifting frontal zones across south-central  FL and a persistent low level ridge axis holding firm just north of the Altamaha River basin. This ridge will limit the northward extend/expansion of the convective band into Sun, and generally focused a W-E oriented swath of heavy rainfall generally near and south of the I-10 corridor overnight into Sunday morning on 6/7.  The ridge strengthened north of the Altamaha Region Sun afternoon 6/7, which shunt the corridor of heavy rain SSW toward the I-75 corridor of NE FL with drier mid level air (700-500 mb) decreasing rain chances across SE GA and eastern NE FL into Sun evening. In addition to heavy rainfall potential, there was a low tornado threat for NE FL into the evening of 6/6 with elevated 0-1 km shear values 20-25 kts with low level ESE winds under increasing  SSW winds out of the GOMEX, with speeds increasing to 35-45 kts at 850 mb through 06z Sun 6/7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station FW5344 measured a 24 hr rainfall of 9.93 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture continued to advect into South Florida while a disturbance aloft moved northward from a broad trough over the Gulf, Yucatan, and western Caribbean. As this disturbance moved northward, a weak surface low formed across the eastern Gulf of Mexico. This diffluent pattern allowed for showers and storms to develop. Due to PWATs in the 2 to 2.4 inch rain, heavy rainfall was expected, and occurred, with flooding across portions of eastern Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS employee reported street flooding in the vicinity of Biscayne Blvd from around NE 11th Street to NE 15th Street due to heavy and prolonged rainfall associated with rain showers. Flood waters exceeding the curb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of May 10, 2020, near and just north of a stationary front extending west-to-east across the Straits of Florida.  Heavy rainfall produced widespread flooding on Long Key including the City of Layton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Secondary roads and parking lots were flooded through most portions of the City of Layton.  Rainfall in excess of 4 inches was estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: FDEM reported that the Hamilton EOC was flooded. Computers, furniture and records on the main floor were damaged. The road department reported that there was $30-$35K of repairs to roadways needed. The cost of damage for the EOC was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level troughing remained prominent across the Gulf of Mexico with a speed max extending east-northeast from the Bay of Campeche into the northwest Caribbean Sea. A decaying Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) was lifting north through the Atlantic waters while surface troughing extended from the southern Florida Peninsula southwest into the southern Gulf of Mexico. Given the diffluent pattern aloft, subtle vorticity perturbations, the remnant MCV, and the surface trough, plentiful sources for lift were evident across South Florida that allowed for the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Additionally, plenty of moisture was distributed areawide with rainfall rates exceeding 2 inches per hour. Abundant moisture along with preceding heavy rainfall and antecedent conditions led to widespread flooding across Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Furthermore, increasing low-level shear behind the MCV whipped up a brief tornado in Miami-Dade County and a funnel cloud in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various reports of flooding received from Broadcast media and members of the public. The public reported water over a curb and halfway up tires of vehicles near the intersection of North Park Road and Johnson Street. Broadcast media reported flooding in the Hollywood area including water on neighborhood streets making travel difficult with cars stalling along with significant flooding near the Hollywood Beach Golf Club with impassable streets. In Hallandale Beach, one family told local media that they woke up to find their ceiling crumbling in a bedroom when the roof collapsed after heavy rain overnight. Another partial roof collapse was reported just a half-mile away in Hollywood. No one was seriously injured. Torrential rainfall produced flooding of poor drainage areas in urban locations as well as near canals and small lakes. A resident in Hollywood told the local news that there was flooding inside and outside their cottage where they noticed water pouring in from the cracks in the floor. The entire floor is concrete and they also saw 3 to 5 inches of water flooding out from underneath the counter and the cabinets in the kitchen. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering frontal boundary across South Florida provided substantial instability for showers and thunderstorms to develop as the Atlantic sea breeze pushed inland. Heavy rainfall associated with these thunderstorms resulted in flooding over Miami-Dade.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off-duty NWS employee reported 4 inches of rainfall that resulted in ponding of water and minor, localized street/parking lot flooding at SW 57th Ave & SW 100th Street, SW 107th Ave & SW 56th St, and at SW 72nd Ave & SW 72nd Street (Sunset Drive/FL 986).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying frontal boundary across South Florida became the focus for convective initiation. Strong thunderstorms developed over the Gulf of Mexico before pushing across the peninsula and then offshore into the Atlantic. These storms produced strong, damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter called to report large tree limbs blown down from thunderstorms which moved through the area. A wind gust of 56 mph was measured around this time at the WeatherBug site at Naples Municipal Airport at a height of 104 feet. Additional estimated wind gusts in the area were in the 60-65 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front was lingering across NE Florida to near the Golden Isles of SE Georgia where PWAT was near 2 inches. Diurnal instability and forcing near the front produced a few strong to pulse severe storms and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near 1153 NW Noggel Road. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The base of an upper level trough was across the SE region. Light steering flow enabled both sea breezes to develop and move inland over a diurnally unstable airmass.  Upper level cool temperatures and dynamical lift enabled a few strong to severe storms along boundary collisions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail was observed near Silver Lake. The time of the event was based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of Cortez Road was washed out due to heavy rainfall.
