EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the early morning hours. These storms produced damaging wind gusts in Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from NBC2 Fort Myers with video from the public of storm damage associated with a thunderstorm with a nearby measured wind gust of 50 mph. Reports of tree limbs down and a lanai had screens torn near the intersection of Brynwood Preserve Lane and Livingston Road in Naples.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry March and beginning of April led to severe drought conditions which developed over interior and western sections of southern Florida by the 3rd week of April. Several wildfires were reported in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts for all of March and the first 3 weeks of April were generally in the 1 inch range across most of inland Collier County. As a result, water levels in the Everglades and Big Cypress Preserve reached near-record low levels. This led to the development of severe drought conditions across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was across the eastern U.S. with a shortwave impulse rotating through the base of the trough across southeast Georgia in the afternoon which support strong to severe storms that developed along sea breeze and outflow boundary storms. The airmass was relatively dry for the Jacksonville area with a precipitable water value of 1.4 inches. Deeper moisture was confined to north central Florida and southward. Shear values included 0-6km shear around 25-30 knots with 500mb temperatures cooling than normal around -9C with dry air aloft. These ingredients favored damaging wind potential in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed at NE 19th Ave in Ocala. The time of the event was based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 43 year old man drowned near the Flora-Bama Lounge.

This is in association with a cluster of thunderstorms with a wind gust of 43 mph/37 knots measured at ASOS station KTMB, Tamiami Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough axis was just east or very near the local Atlantic coast with weaker bulk shear in the 0-6 km layer around 30 kts with drier air in place which created strong down drafts in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down over a driveway. The time of damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was unknown but estimated for Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reported photos of tree split, with large branch down near SW 8th street and SW 127th ave in Miami as a thunderstorm. Also reported was a damage to a thin metal carport and associated tarp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving north across South Florida, along with a warm and moist local atmosphere, provided support for the development of thunderstorms. Several outflow boundary collisions from nearby storms helped to produce a short-lived tornado in Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey team found damage consistent with an EF-0 tornado in the San Castle neighborhood in Palm Beach County. This area is east of I-95 tucked right between the Boynton Beach and Hypoluxo city limits. Track began near Miner Road and Summit Road, and tracked N-NE between Summit and Grove Road, with the heaviest damage along Brown Road. The track then shifted east of Summit Road from Wilkinson Road to Overlook Road where it lifted near Monroe Boulevard. ||Damage consisted of multiple downed tree limbs and minor damage to home roofs and fences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Mexico helped to pump moist and unstable air across South Florida during the evening and nighttime hours of January 31st, and early morning of February 1st. Strong thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front over the eastern Gulf of Mexico, and moved east across the southern Florida peninsula. As these storms moved across Miami Beach, heavy rainfall and minor flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several social media reports showed street flooding due to heavy rainfall in Miami Beach. An off duty NWS employee reported minor flooding in Wynwood near the intersection of NW 2nd Ave and 27th Terr. Pictures and video via social media showed water ankle high and flowing, along with stranded cars and floating barricades due to excessive water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong speed shear preceded a potent storm system on February 6th. By early afternoon, widespread wind gusts of 40-47 mph from the SSW were reported across NE Florida through about 5 pm which caused tree damage and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts of 40-50 mph were reported across the area. Wind damage including trees down and power line damage was reported in Baker by a storm spotter around 3 pm. Pine limbs littered the roads in Macclenny from 5th Street to US Highway 90 South. There were power lines down near Woodlawn Road and Woodlawn Cemetery. At 4 pm, the spotter reported a tree blown down on power lines near Jeff Starling Road and State Road 121 about 2 miles S of Macclenny. There was also a tree down across Noel Road and Kyle Pen Road about 5 miles SE of Taylor. The cost of damage was unknown but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent pref-frontal squall line crossed the local forecast area during the late afternoon across SE Georgia and continuing eastward exiting the NE Florida Atlantic coast through midnight.  There was very strong speed shear over the local area with strong 0-6km shear around 55 knots, 0-1km helicity over 300 m2/s2, and sufficient CAPE for severe weather. The limiting factor was fairly weak mid-level lapse rates. Widespread wind damage occurred with this line with gusts of 40-50 mph and severe strength bowing segments producing wind damage and gusts of 60-76 mph. A isolated tornado briefly touched down in Waycross.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage was reported 2 miles SE of Glen St. Mary with 3-inch diameter tree limbs broken and power poles broken. Time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Veering surface flow, from the south then southwest, ahead of an approaching cold front allowed for plenty of moisture to advect in across the region. Sufficient 0-3km bulk shear flowing parallel to the showers/storms supported training of this activity. As the frontal boundary approached the region in the early afternoon to the overnight hours, showers and storms developed along the front. As these showers/storms moved across the east coast, especially for Palm Beach County, heavy rainfall and minor flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several social media reports showed street flooding due to heavy rainfall in portions of Palm Beach County, especially for Delray Beach and surrounding areas. Pictures and video via social media showed excessive water on roadways and at least a few inches deep while flowing where additional pictures/videos of water entering a residential or retail plaza yard with standing water around tables.||Rainfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 3 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level short wave trough energy tracked across the region and combined with intense daytime heating and abundant moisture produced thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The best instability resided along and southeast of line from  Homerville to Ponte Vedra where CAPES were maximized near 3000-3700 J/kg in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was observed near 28th Street in Flagler Beach. The convection moved east over the ocean and the funnel dissipated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station along Aysrshire Street recorded 2.86 inches of rain in about 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very high moisture with PWAT 2.10-2.20 inches lingered across the region trailing departing low pressure tracking north, offshore of the mid-Atlantic coast. This moisture combined with sea breeze, diurnal heating and a convergent boundary across north-central FL enabled areas of heavy rainfall in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Robinson Drive in Palm Coast had 5 inches of standing water on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: WNW steering flow continued across the area with a ridge of high pressure across the southern FL peninsula from the central Gulf and low pressure to our northeast |tracking northward along the mid Atlantic seaboard. High moisture content with PW over 2 inches combined with diurnal instability and seas breezes and fairly warm mid levels (500 mb -5 degC), locally heavy rainfall occurred in evening storms near the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.47 inches was measured in 45 minutes in East Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A transition to a drier airmass in the mid/upper levels occurred with precipitable water amounts (PWAT) falling to 1.2-1.6 inches across much of the area except for a lingering high plume of 2 inches across parts of NE FL. This moisture combined with a weak east coast sea breeze triggered scattered to numerous storms across the I-95 corridor, St Johns River Basin and US 301 corridor in the afternoon and evening. Warm temperatures/weak lapse rates limited severe storm potential, but a few strong storms with wet downbursts did produce some damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-southwesterly flow allowed for deep tropical moisture to stay in place across South Florida. This prevailing flow also allowed the Gulf sea breeze to progress further inland, pinning the Atlantic sea breeze to the coast, becoming the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms. The presence of a mid-level trough and attendant short wave pushing through South Florida allowed for an increase in thunderstorms along the east coast that brought gusty winds, heavy rainfall, flooding, and hail to portions of Broward, Palm Beach, and Miami-Dade counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from social media of some trees down in the Hollywood area. Picture evidence of a large tree branch snapped and leaning over roadway. Also, light, thin banners originally attached to a fence of Hollywood Hills High School were blown off. Wind gust estimated from the HWO ASOS of 46 mph surrounding the time frame of the report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both a NWS employee and members of the public reported flooding in the Palm Beach County area. Traffic reports of flooding in the vicinity of Interstate 95 South and west of Clear Lake. Also, a NWS employee reported a parking lot flooded near the intersection of South Military Trail/Florida Highway 809 and Summit Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta offshore of the SW FL Gulf Coast tracked NE through the day, crossing NE FL while gradually weakening through the day. Strong, gusty winds and locally heavy rainfall impacted NE FL as bands of heavy showers moved onshore. The storm moved offshore of the NE FL coast in the afternoon, with improving local conditions over night. Elevated tides combined with persistent, strong onshore flow caused isolated minor flooding within the St. Johns River basin during times of high tide. Water levels were generally near or just below 1.5 feet MHHW datum at high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was no significant damage reported across Duval County. Peak measured winds and wind gusts included the following observations: on 11/11 at 8:10 pm, a Weatherflow site on the St. Johns River downtown Terminal Channel recorded 40 mph (116 deg) with a peak guts of 50 mph. On 11/12 at 7:47 am, a Weatherflow site on Buck Island in the St. Johns River east of Mayport measured a peak wind of 40 mph (158 deg) with a gust of 47 (158 deg) at 7:46 am. On 11/12 at 7:53 am, Craig Airfield ASOS measured 25 mph (160 deg) with a gust of 46 mph. On 11/12 at 8:14 am, the Weatherflow station at Huguenot Park measured 45 mph (147 deg) with a peak gust of 52 mph (148 deg) at 8:12 am. At the Jacksonville International Airport ASOS on 11/12 at 8:16 am, the peak wind was 22 mph (130 deg) with a gust of 33 mph (130 deg) at 8:16 am. Marine observation from the NOS Station at Mayport included 20 mph (140 deg) on 11/12 at 9:30 am with a peak gust of 36 mph (140 deg). Maximum water levels included 1.43 ft MHHW datum at the Mayport tide gage on 11/09 at 3:06 pm and 1.77 ft MHHW at the Main Street Bridge tide gage on the St. Johns River in downtown Jacksonville on 11/12 at 9 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta moved across Cuba and into the Florida Straits on the morning of November 8. Eta tracked northwest through the day and then made landfall along the Central Florida Keys early in the morning of November 9. Outer rain bands on the northern side of Eta's circulation moved onshore of the east-central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds along the barrier islands from Brevard to Martin Counties, and gusts to tropical storm force west of the Intracoastal Waterway. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from Eta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Indian River County, sustained tropical storm force winds affected the barrier islands, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county from mid evening on November 8 through early morning on November 9. The highest sustained wind was measured at Indian River Shores (41 mph from the east-northeast at 1951LST on November 8) and the peak gust was also at Indian River Shores (58 mph from the east-northeast at 1951LST on November 8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta moved across Cuba and into the Florida Straits on the morning of November 8. Eta tracked northwest through the day and then made landfall along the Central Florida Keys early in the morning of November 9. Outer rain bands on the northern side of Eta's circulation moved onshore of the east-central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds along the barrier islands from Brevard to Martin Counties, and gusts to tropical storm force west of the Intracoastal Waterway. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from Eta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Brevard County, sustained tropical storm force winds affected the barrier islands, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county from late evening on November 8 through late morning on November 9. The highest sustained wind was measured at Melbourne Beach (44 mph from the east-northeast at 0114LST on November 9) and the peak gust was also at Melbourne Beach (55 mph from the east-northeast at 0114LST on November 9).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough axis extended from offshore of the local Atlantic SSW across the southern FL peninsula in the morning and westward toward TS Marco in the Gulf of Mexico. The trough lifted northward and meandered across SE GA in the afternoon with very high tropical moisture over the local area (PWAT over 2 inches). The surface trough axis combined with daytime heating, sea breezes and weak upper level PVA focused the best potential for a few strong to severe afternoon storms over inland areas as well as minor flood potential under ENE steering flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in NW Live Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough axis extended from offshore of the local Atlantic SSW across the southern FL peninsula in the morning and westward toward TS Marco in the Gulf of Mexico. The trough lifted northward and meandered across SE GA in the afternoon with very high tropical moisture over the local area (PWAT over 2 inches). The surface trough axis combined with daytime heating, sea breezes and weak upper level PVA focused the best potential for a few strong to severe afternoon storms over inland areas as well as minor flood potential under ENE steering flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near County Road 152 between Jennings and Jasper. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta moved across Cuba and into the Florida Straits on the morning of November 8. Eta tracked northwest through the day and then made landfall along the Central Florida Keys early in the morning of November 9. Outer rain bands on the northern side of Eta's circulation moved onshore of the east-central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds along the barrier islands from Brevard to Martin Counties, and gusts to tropical storm force west of the Intracoastal Waterway. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from Eta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Okeechobee County, sustained winds remained below tropical storm force with the strongest winds occurring in outer rain bands. The highest peak wind gust measured was at the Okeechobee County Airport (32 mph from the east at 2155LST on November 8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the sea breeze in the late afternoon over the interior and pushed towards the East Coast due to prevailing southerly/southwesterly flow. As these storms developed in the interior during peak heating hours, they produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video sent via social media showed a funnel cloud produced by a thunderstorm in South Bay, FL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong waterspout came ashore near Bradenton beach producing a tornado. A lifeguard tower was close to the tornado and produced a strong wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout came ashore near Bradenton Beach producing a tornado. Damage included a lifeguard stand, an outdoor wooden canopy with picnic table, half a dozen umbrellas stands, and medium size tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong waterspout came ashore near Bradenton beach producing a tornado. A lifeguard tower was close to the tornado and produced a strong wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguard stand reported a strong wind gust as waterspout came ashore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak downburst from a collapsing thunderstorm produces damage at a baseball diamond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak microburst from a collapsing thunderstorm produced sub severe wind gust that damaged a batting cage and a bleacher at a baseball diamond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta offshore of the SW FL Gulf Coast tracked NE through the day, crossing NE FL while gradually weakening through the day. Strong, gusty winds and locally heavy rainfall impacted NE FL as bands of heavy showers moved onshore. The storm moved offshore of the NE FL coast in the afternoon, with improving local conditions over night. Elevated tides combined with persistent, strong onshore flow caused isolated minor flooding within the St. Johns River basin during times of high tide. Water levels were generally near or just below 1.5 feet MHHW datum at high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage was not reported. On 12/12 at 6:53 am, the AWOS at Naval Air Station Jacksonville measured 24 mph (140 deg) with a peak gust of 39 mph (150 deg) at 6:56 am.

At 4 am on 11/12, a FAWN mesonet site in the city of Alachua measured a wind gust of 21 mph (139 deg) with a peak gust of 43 mph (139 deg).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the sea breeze in the late afternoon over the interior and pushed towards the East Coast due to prevailing southerly/southwesterly flow. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed reports of an EF0 landspout tornado produced by a thunderstorm outflow boundary collision near Haverhill, FL. The landspout persisted for a few minutes. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak easterly flow allowed for deep tropical moisture to stay in place across South Florida. This prevailing flow also allowed the Gulf and Atlantic sea breezes to push inland, becoming the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Due to weak flow, these sea breeze boundaries were slow to move. This allowed for gusty showers and thunderstorms along the east coast that brought gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EarthNetworks sensor site WSTNN at Cypress Bay High School reported a 66 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong seabreeze thunderstorm in Polk County produced penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported penny size hail near Crystal Lake in Polk County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm in Lee county produced weak rotation and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media shared a picture of a funnel cloud near RSW airport. There is no evidence it touched the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimal storm impacts were reported across Nassau county. At 9:36 am on 11/12, the NOS Station at Fernandina Beach measured a peak wind of 22 mph (150 deg) with a peak gust of 38 mph. The highest water level was measured days before peak winds on 11/09 at 3:42 pm of 1.08 ft MHHW datum at the same NOS station (still below action stage for this site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-southwesterly flow allowed for deep tropical moisture to stay in place across South Florida. This prevailing flow also allowed the Gulf sea breeze to progress further inland, pinning the Atlantic sea breeze to the coast, becoming the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms. With the presence of a mid-level trough across the NE CONUS, its southern edges influenced South Florida, which allowed for an increase in thunderstorms along the east coast that brought gusty winds and hail to portions of Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported quarter size hail on Interstate 595 in Davie.

One of these homes suffered severe roof damage, with part of the roof detached and blown off. Estimated winds based on the damage observed as well as data from TFLL radar is around 75 mph. This damage is a result of downburst winds and not tornadic. Damage amount is also estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly flow allowed for showers and storms to develop and migrate towards the Gulf Coast of South Florida. An inverted trough axis that finally pushed from the Florida peninsula and out in the Gulf Of Mexico further allowed for instability and aided in the development of convection, especially with a nearly cloud-free morning. Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall eventually led to flooding in portions of Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported flooding across Marco Island associated with thunderstorms that slowly pushed through the area. Reports that multiple streets were under 1-2 feet of water with gauge reports across Marco Island measuring between 3 to 6 inches of rainfall in only a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-southwesterly flow allowed for deep tropical moisture to stay in place across South Florida. This prevailing flow also allowed the Gulf sea breeze to progress further inland, pinning the Atlantic sea breeze to the coast, becoming the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Broad high pressure remained in control, though a weak low pressure lingered around the MS/AL coastline with an associated surface boundary stretching from east TX through the FL panhandle and into the western Atlantic. This allowed for an increase in thunderstorms along the east coast that brought gusty winds and hail to portions of Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from MPING: Penny size hail from thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several low level boundaries were across the local forecast area including a wake trough axis post-Isaias across south Georgia and an outflow boundary stalled near the I-95 corridor of NE FL. With daytime heating, high moisture content (PWAT 2 inches) and steeper mid level lapse rates (MU 7.4 degC), scattered strong to severe storms developed along and near the boundaries. Hazards included downbursts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McCoys creek flooded onto McCoys Creek Blvd. Some of the deepest water was at the King Street intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta passed northwest through the middle Florida Keys on November 8 through 9, 2020.  Primary impacts from wind occurred as result of a primary rain band which drifted very slowly north through the Upper Florida Keys.  Peak wind gusts were mostly in the 50 to 60 mph range, downing trees and large tree limbs, utility lines, and a billboard. One residence was destroyed by a fallen tree resulting in 1 minor injury. Two other residential structures were significantly damaged, with three inaccessible  due to blocked streets. About 20 separate power outages affected about 1,000 customers, mostly in Key Largo. Storm Surge flooding was generally in the 1.5 to 2.5 foot range, producing significant street flooding in oceanside Key Largo neighborhoods.  The northbound right-of-way was damaged including major erosion, loss of riprap, and about 3,000 feet of fencing along northbound U.S. Highway 1 along the west end of Barnes Sound and Manatee Bay.  Minor overwash occurred in the middle Florida Keys at Key Colony Beach, along with yard and dock flooding along the Florida Bay side of Marathon. Widespread rainfall of 3.5 to over 8 inches was measured throughout the Keys, with the highest totals in Key Largo.  Damages were estimated near $500K due to mostly from homes damaged by falling trees and tree limbs, as well as utility damage. Storm Surge damage near $100K due to wave action on top of a minor storm surge to the U.S Highway 1 right-of-way.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 40 mph with frequent gusts of 40 to 60 mph were observed throughout the upper Florida Keys, with the strongest winds measured at a sustained speed of 41 mph at a private residential development in Rock Harbor with a peak gust of 58 mph. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were also measured at the Florida Keys Electric Cooperative Substation at Jewfish Creek, as well as at a residence on Plantation Key. Numerous trees and power lines were downed, with one house destroyed by a fallen tree in Key Largo along with one injury.  Approximately 1,000 customers were without power, mostly in Key Largo.  One wooden-post billboard was downed, with County Road 905, also known as Card Sound Road, blocked in several places due to downed trees between mile markers 3 and 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the area.  One storm produced a tornado as it moved through Madison county with some damage, resulting in damage to farm buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blocked the right lane and partially the left lane of I-10 eastbound near mile marker 236.

This allowed for an increase in thunderstorms along the east coast that brought gusty winds and hail to portions of Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a large outdoor contraption, consisting of mesh netting well-attached to a solid wooden enclosure, was fully lofted and tilted vertically, colliding with backyard facing side of a house. Mesh was damaged. Backyard lawn recreational items also toppled. This thunderstorm wind damage was in association with a microburst. Estimated wind gusts near 60 mph based primarily on magnitude of damage as well as strength of storm observed on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall containing severe wind gusts passed through portions of the Lower Florida Keys, with maximum wind gusts occurring on Cudjoe Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 50 knots, or 58 mph, was measured on Cudjoe Key near the southern end of Sawyer Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter called the NWS Miami to report quarter size hail at the intersection of Belvedere Road and Interstate 95. This was associated with a weak-supercell storm that crossed the West Palm Beach area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Tropical Storm Eta continues to approach the FL coast in the NE Gulf of Mexico, bands of trailing showers and embedded thunderstorms spread across South Florida from the SSW to the NNE. Some of these bands contained heavy rain and gusty winds. With generally SSW steering flow in place, brief periods of training convection occurred. Due to super saturated antecedent conditions, minimal rain contributed to flooding across the east coast of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Miami Herald reporter and members of the public posted videos of significant flooding in the Biscayne Point, Normandy Isles, and North Beach neighborhoods of Miami Beach.  Streets were impassable and several cars were stranded. Water also encroached on several homes. ||Rain gauges in the area measured between 3.6 and 4.7 inches of rain in 3 hours between 5:15 PM and 8:15 PM, with daily totals in the 4-6 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection was over inland areas near both inland advancing sea breezes and along a SW-NE oriented boundary across SE GA. High moisture content, slow SW storm motion near 10 kts, steeper mid level lapse rates and cooler mid level temps (500 mb -6.7 degC) all supported strong to pulse severe storm potential along boundary collisions between Highway 301 and the St. Johns River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Pringle Court. The time of damage was based on radar.

Wave action estimated around 4 feet produced severe erosion on along the northbound right-of-way of U.S. Highway 1 south of Manatee Bay, destroying 3,000 feet of chain link fence as well as producing potholes and loss of protective riprap.

Time of report is based on radar data showing a strengthening storm following the initial strong wind gust of 55 mph measured by KPBI ASOS at the airport nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-southwesterly flow allowed for deep tropical moisture to stay in place across South Florida. This prevailing flow also allowed the Gulf sea breeze to progress further inland, pinning the Atlantic sea breeze to the coast, becoming the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms. The presence of a mid-level trough and attendant short wave pushing through South Florida allowed for an increase in thunderstorms along the east coast that brought gusty winds, heavy rainfall, flooding, and hail to portions of Broward, Palm Beach, and Miami-Dade counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported nickel size hail on Southern Boulevard in eastern parts of West Palm Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breezes merged near the St. Johns river basin over moist and unstable airmass, with cooler mid level temperatures around -7 degC at 500 mb. A passing mid level short wave trough enhanced lift over the sea breeze collision, with very slow storm motion to the ESE less than 10 mph enabling slow moving, heavy rain producing cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 852 pm, the broadcast media relayed a video of flooding along College Street in Riverside. Cars appeared they were driving through about 1 ft of flood water. At 918 pm, the public reported water flowing over the road at Roosevelt Blvd and Hollywood in Jacksonville. At 924 pm, broadcast media reported cars were trapped in flood water along College Drive and Acosta Steers in Riverside. At 930 pm, the public reported flooding across the road at Fitch and Belvedere in Jacksonville.  At 1025 pm, the public reported flooding at Pine Grove Avenue in Avondale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta moved northeast and gradually intensified west of Dry Tortugas.  An expanding wind field brought tropical-storm force wind gusts to the extreme Lower Keys and Dry Tortugas.  While water levels remained below coastal flood thresholds, heavy wave action produced extensive overwash along the oceanfront of Key West, resulting in closed streets.  Minor power outages occurred primarily in Key West.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Tropical Storm Eta moved to the north northeast well to the west of the Lower Florida Keys, sustained winds of 25 to 30 mph with frequent gusts of 40 to 45 mph occurred.  Peak gusts of 44 mph were measured at Key West High School, and 43 mph at Cudjoe Key.  Minor power outages affecting less than 100 customers occurred in Key West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep and light west southwest flow was over the area which pushed a more dominant west coast sea breeze across the area. Temperatures aloft cooled to -8C at 500mb. Deep moisture remained over the area with precipitable water levels near 2 inches. There was not a lot of shear which favored pulse summertime convection. A few strong to pulse severe storms formed along the sea breeze merger near the I-95 corridor in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads had standing water on them and one road was down to one lane due to flood water in Palatka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta moved across Cuba and into the Florida Straits on the morning of November 8. Eta tracked northwest through the day and then made landfall along the Central Florida Keys early in the morning of November 9. Outer rain bands on the northern side of Eta's circulation moved onshore of the east-central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds along the barrier islands from Brevard to Martin Counties, and gusts to tropical storm force west of the Intracoastal Waterway. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from Eta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Martin County, sustained tropical storm force winds affected the barrier islands, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county from early evening on November 8 through mid morning on November 9. The highest sustained wind was measured at Sailfish Point in Stuart (39 mph from the east at 0230LST on November 9) and the peak gust was at the House of Refuge in Stuart (64 mph from the east-southeast at 2139LST on November 8).

The highest sustained wind was measured at the Saint Lucie Nuclear Power Plant (48 mph from the east-northeast at 1842LST on November 8) and the peak gust was also at the Saint Lucie Nuclear Power Plant (60 mph from the east-northeast at 1842LST on November 8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer WSW steering flow was light across NE which enable sea breezes and river breezes to converge over inland areas over an unstable airmass. Cool mid level temperatures at 500 mb ranged from -10 to -12 deg C. This cool air aloft combined with high diurnal instability produced a few strong to severe storms with hail across NE FL. Strong WSW steering flow across SE GA produced a few strong wind gusts under stronger upper level dynamics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail was reported along Eagle Landing Parkway in Oakleaf Plantation. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse surface trough axis was north of the Altamaha River basin with very high dew pts in the low/mid 70s across the entire forecast area south of the boundary. The 12z|JAX RAOB showed less deep layer moisture (PWAT 1.6 inches) and stronger SW steering flow across the entire area from 10-15 kts south of I-10 across NE FL to 20 kts across our SE GA zones. There was drier air in the low/mid levels where the steepest lapse rates were near 6.8 C/km between 790 mb and 617 mb roughly. SE GA 0-6 km bulk shear was 20-25 kts. Ingredients favored pulse strong to severe storms with gusty downburst winds the main hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherStem Station at the Gilchrist County Fire Station 4 measured 3.38 inches of daily rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 312 pm, a brush fire started when an unauthorized pile burn became out of control and burned 17 acres and burned a tractor at 20505 NE 22nd Lane.  The fire was 100% contained at 17.5 acres. Three house sustained damage of unknown severity and Alachua Sheriff conducted door-to-door evacuations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 312 pm, a brush fire started when an unauthorized pile burn became out of control and burned 17 acres and burned a tractor at 20505 NE 22nd Lane. The fire was 100% contained at 17.5 acres. Three house sustained damage of unknown severity and Alachua Sheriff conducted door-to-door evacuations. The cost of the tractor was estimated and included in Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A St George Island volunteer fire department first responder died while trying to save a father and son in distress from rip currents.  Double red flags were flying.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prevailing light, east-southeasterly flow allowed for both the Gulf  and Atlantic sea breeze to progress inland through peak heating hours. Drier air aloft centered over the Bahamas worked in concert with steepening low-level lapses to produce sufficient accent for showers and thunderstorms to develop within the saturated airmass. Collisions between the sea breeze boundaries and additional outflow boundaries resulted in a few stronger storms that produced a funnel cloud over Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video received via social media of a funnel cloud in rural western Palm Beach County, roughly 9 miles NW of Lion Country Safari. Location is approximate based on radar data. No reports of damage received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving across the tropical Atlantic into the eastern Caribbean Sea formed into Tropical Storm Isaias on July 28th. While the system briefly became a hurricane near Great Inagua Island on July 30th, it was downgraded back to a Tropical Storm as it moved over Andros Island on August 1st with a minimum central pressure of 993 mb. Isaias gradually turned to the N-NW and the center passed about 30-40 miles east of the Palm Beach County coast during the morning of August 2nd with a minimum central pressure of 995 mb and maximum sustained winds of 65 to 70 mph. Lowest surface pressure measured over South Florida was 1003.1 mb at Lake Worth Pier on August 2nd. ||Although the effects from Isaias were minor for South Florida, sustained Tropical Storm force winds were felt across portions of Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade Counties. Rainfall flooding impacts were minor across South Florida with 2 to 2.6 inches measured mainly across Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Almost 3,000 customers lost power during the event, almost all of them in Palm Beach County. ||A peak storm surge of 1.11 feet was observed at Lake Worth Pier, 1.06 feet at Virginia Key, and 0.99 feet at Port Everglades. Along the Atlantic coast, minor to moderate beach erosion was observed in usually vulnerable locations, most notably at the beaches of Palm Beach and Broward counties. A total of 165 people voluntarily evacuated in Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Isaias moved offshore across the South Florida waters, outer rain bands produced  produced maximum sustained winds generally between 25-30 mph. A tropical storm-force wind gust of 39 mph / 34 knots was measured by the AWOS station KF45 North Palm Beach was recorded at 4:25 PM EDT on August 1st. Wind impacts were minor and confined to trees and other vegetation. Total number of customers without power is included in the event entries for Coastal and Metro Palm Beach County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms over Florida Bay moved south, producing an isolated severe thunderstorm wind gust near Crawl Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was measured at an automated WeatherFlow station near Crawl Key.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze storms merged near the St. Johns River basin over a moist and unstable airmass. SW steering flow pushed the storms offshore of the local Atlantic coast in the late afternoon.  Drier mid level air above 700 mb elevated downdraft CAPE to over 1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto power lines at the intersection of Arlington Road and Whitlock Avenue. The time of damage was based on radar. Observations just west of this location had measured winds of 49-53 mph.  The cost of damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree debris was blown down onto power lines that damaged FPL equipment. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved across the local area during the pre-dawn hours. Dynamical forcing was strong including deep layer shear and low level helicity values supportive of strong downburst wind and isolated tornadoes, but instability was overall lacking across the local area to support a widespread severe storm threat. A nose of instability just ahead of the squall line edged inland across portions of the Suwannee River Valley during the early morning hours, which phased with stronger dynamics with the squall lien and produced a few strong to severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near White Springs. The time of damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for Storm Data.

Wind gusts to tropical storm force were measured as high as 39-53 mph. The highest measured tropical storm-force wind gust was 53 mph (46 kt) by ASOS station KPBI West Palm Beach recorded at 4:08 PM EDT on August 1st. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was measured at the rooftop of the National Weather Service Florida Keys in Key West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust, stationary trough over the Gulf of Mexico kept deep, south/southwest flow and rich tropical moisture over South Florida. Several short waves rotating |around the base of the trough enhanced the showers and |thunderstorms that produced a tornado over the Golden Beach area in Miami-Dade from a waterspouts and flooding from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video on social media showed street flooding east of I-95 on Forest Hill Blvd and Forest Court, near Forest Hill High School. Surrounding area received around 3 to 5 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread tropical storm force wind gusts occurred association with Hurricane Laura, as it passed well south and west of the Florida Keys as a tropical storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Laura, while still at tropical storm intensity, passed well south and southwest of the Florida Keys while tracking just off the south coast of Cuba, then turning northwest and crossing western Cuba west of Havana.  Sustained tropical storm force winds peaked at 34 knots (39 mph) at Key West International Airport, with a peak gust of 41 knots (47 mph).  Other notable peak wind gusts in the Lower Keys of Monroe County included a measured gust of 60 knots (69 mph) at the National Weather Service Office in Key West, 49 knots (56 mph) at the Keys Energy Service Building in downtown Key West, and 48 knots (55 mph) at the U.S. Coast Guard Sector in Key West.  Damage was limited to tree limbs and one downed medium-sized schefflera actinophylla (umbrella tree) in Key West.  Wind damage was estimated at about $2.5K.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isaias tracked offshore of the local forecast area through the day August 3rd.  Occasional rainbands moved onshore over the local area through the day with gusts of 20-40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: TS Isaias tracked north, offshore of the local coast. Broken rain bands moved onshore with brief squalls. At  5:27 am on 8/3, the AWOS at St. Augustine airport measured a peak wind of 21 mph from the north. A peak gust of 32 mph from the NNE was measured at 5:59 am. The St. Augustine C-Man station measured peak winds at 6:10 am with sustained 33 mph and a gust to 41 mph. Rainfall was minimal across the area with measured totals 1.02 inches at a few locations across western parts of the county per a CoCoRaHS station. Otherwise, rainfall was less than 1 inch with several areas near St. Augustine measuring around 0.50 inches. Storm surge was up to 2 ft inundation, most locations were 1.5 ft above dry ground or less. The only damage reported was from the National Park Service who stated that there was minor beach erosion at Fort Matanzas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isaias tracked offshore of the local forecast area through the day August 3rd.  Occasional rainbands moved onshore over the local area through the day with gusts of 20-40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northward and remained offshore of the local Atlantic coast and produced with broken squalls moving onshore over the local area. Impacts to the county were minimal and confined to the immediate coast where Jacksonville Beach lost about 5 feet of sand in some locations. The peak wind across the county was measured with a WeatherFlow mesonet station at Jacksonville Beach Pier and was sustained 35 mph with a peak gust of 41 mph on Aug. 3rd at 3:27 pm. Gusts in squalls generally ranged between 30-40 mph elsewhere across the county. Rainfall was minimal and the peak value across the county was measured at the NWS Jacksonville office of 0.53 inches. Peak storm surged was 1.44 ft above ground level (MHHW) at Mayport on Aug 3rd at 9:54 pm. The surge reached 1.23 ft MHHW at the Dames Point Bridge at 11 pm on Aug 3rd. Within the St Johns River basin peak surge reach 0.88 ft MHHW at the Southbank Riverwalk in downtown Jacksonville on Aug 3rd at 11:45 pm and 0.80 ft MHHW at the Buckman Bridge on Aug  4th at 12:45 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving across the tropical Atlantic into the eastern Caribbean Sea formed into Tropical Storm Isaias on July 28th. While the system briefly became a hurricane near Great Inagua Island on July 30th, it was downgraded back to a Tropical Storm as it moved over Andros Island on August 1st with a minimum central pressure of 993 mb. Isaias gradually turned to the N-NW and the center passed about 30-40 miles east of the Palm Beach County coast during the morning of August 2nd with a minimum central pressure of 995 mb and maximum sustained winds of 65 to 70 mph. Lowest surface pressure measured over South Florida was 1003.1 mb at Lake Worth Pier on August 2nd. ||Although the effects from Isaias were minor for South Florida, sustained Tropical Storm force winds were felt across portions of Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade Counties. Rainfall flooding impacts were minor across South Florida with 2 to 2.6 inches measured mainly across Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Almost 3,000 customers lost power during the event, almost all of them in Palm Beach County. ||A peak storm surge of 1.11 feet was observed at Lake Worth Pier, 1.06 feet at Virginia Key, and 0.99 feet at Port Everglades. Along the Atlantic coast, minor to moderate beach erosion was observed in usually vulnerable locations, most notably at the beaches of Palm Beach and Broward counties. A total of 165 people voluntarily evacuated in Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Isaias moved offshore across the South Florida waters, outer rain bands produced produced maximum sustained winds generally between 25-30 mph. A tropical storm-force wind gust of 40 mph / 35 knots was recorded by the ASOS station KHWO, Pembroke Pines at 2:35 PM EDT on August 1st. Wind impacts were minor and confined to trees and other vegetation. A total of around 190 customers lost power in Broward County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving across the tropical Atlantic into the Bahamas formed into Tropical Storm Laura near Puerto Rico and Hispaniola. As Laura continued across the northern Caribbean Sea, making landfall along southern Cuba, the outer rain bands extended across the South Florida bringing minor impacts. Tropical Storm force wind gusts reached across Miami-Dade, a few strong enough to become severe gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm wind gust of 65mph / 56 knots was recorded by the Wxflow Mesonet site, XTKY Turkey point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving early morning thunderstorms produced some flash flooding across Panama City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in Panama City were reported underwater via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved across the local area during the pre-dawn hours. Dynamical forcing was strong including deep layer shear and low level helicity values supportive of strong downburst wind and isolated tornadoes, but instability was overall lacking across the local area to support a widespread severe storm threat. A nose of instability just ahead of the squall line edged inland across portions of the Suwannee River Valley during the early morning hours, which phased with stronger dynamics with the squall lien and produced a few strong to severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey determined that straight line winds near 80 mph caused widespread tree damage. Some trees damaged structures. The worst damage was centered near the intersection of NW 39th Ave and Highway 222 and NW 34th Street and Highway 121. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough progressing across the area from the Gulf of Mexico enhanced convective coverage across South Florida. Furthermore, south/southwesterly flow kept ample moisture in place, while helping push the Gulf sea breeze boundary, pinning the Atlantic sea breeze across the metro areas. A few storms across Broward and Miami-Dade counties became strong enough to produce damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Power and Light utility crews reported wind damage to trees in West Park  associated with a strong thunderstorm that moved across the area. Wind speeds estimated to be near 60 mph based on the damage. A nearby Earth Networks weather station at Watkins Elementary School recorded a 51 mph (44 knots) wind gust at 6:43 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The inland advancing dominant east coast sea breeze triggered a strong storm with a gusty wet down burst that caused some minor wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet down burst caused some minor wind damage including breaking off tree limbs of 4 inches or less in diameter. The cost of damage was unknown but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isaias tracked offshore of the local forecast area through the day August 3rd.  Occasional rainbands moved onshore over the local area through the day with gusts of 20-40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimal impacts occurred to the county. The peak wind measured at the Fernandina Beach AWOS was 20 mph with a gust to 29 mph on Aug. 3rd at 2:35 pm. Measured rainfall was less than 0.50 inches across the county. The peak surge at Fernandina Beach was 1.59 ft MHHW on Aug. 3rd at 10:18 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isaias tracked offshore of the local forecast area through the day August 3rd.  Occasional rainbands moved onshore over the local area through the day with gusts of 20-40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was no significant damage to the county. The peak wind gust was 41 mph at the Marineland WeatherFlow station on August 3rd at 3:23 am. Peak measured storm total rainfall was 0.63 inches about 1 mile SE of Flagler Beach by a COCORaHS observer. Peak storm surge was 1.22 ft  MHHW at Mantanzas River Bings Landing on August 4th at 1 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused damage in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed was damaged by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Troughing across the eastern CONUS and the subtropical ridge worked in concert to aid S/SW flow in lofting a continuous plume of tropical moisture across South Florida in the wake of Isaias as it recurved towards the NE. Given sufficient daytime heating, scattered thunderstorms developed across the east coast metro, one producing lightning that striked a construction worker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 10 News reported that Lauderhill Fire-Rescue responded to a construction worker working at Castle Hill Elementary who had been struck by lightning. Other workers at the school reported that the man was thrown about 15 feet by the lightning strike. He was taken to the hospital, but thankfully, listed in stable condition.||Time of the event is based on lightning strike archive data courtesy of Vaisala's lightning detection network which showed a cloud-to-ground strike in close proximity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low in south Florida helped to increase our typical summer time convection which caused some flooding in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media shared a picture of a residential road completely under water in Whiskey Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere combined with a weak upper level disturbance led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms became strong to severe with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees across a power line on Howell Road near CR 97.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere combined with a weak upper level disturbance led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms became strong to severe with strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was hit by lightning and sustained heavy damage due to fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient tropical moisture across South Florida in the wake of Isaias and ample daytime heating allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop across the east coast metro as the Gulf Coast sea Breeze collided with the semi-pinned Atlantic sea Breeze. One storm produced localized flooding over Downtown Miami.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media post showed about 3 to 4 inches of standing water on roadways in Brickell near the Publix located near the Intersection of SW 7th St and SW 3rd Ave in association with thunderstorms that moved through the area. Time estimated by radar to be around 8 to 8:30pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light southerly flow across South Florida allowed for both the Atlantic and Gulf sea breezes to develop, becoming the focus of afternoon convection. Additionally, a mid-upper level trough extending from Canada supported enhanced coverage of thunderstorms along the east coast, which brought gusty winds and hail to Broward and Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported via social media a thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph (54 knots) that was measured by a home weather station in Loxahatchee. The post also mentioned the downburst associated with the passing thunderstorm, resulting in some downed trees and branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light southerly flow across South Florida allowed for both the Atlantic and Gulf sea breezes to develop, becoming the focus of afternoon convection. Additionally, a mid-upper level trough extending from Canada supported enhanced coverage of thunderstorms along the east coast, which brought gusty winds and hail to Broward and Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported several large tree branches down in the vicinity of SW 178th Ave and SW 61st Ct in Southwest Ranches. A wind gust of 59 mph was measured by the Earth Networks site at Cypress Bay High School, about 1.5 miles to the NNW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low in south Florida helped to increase our typical summer time convection which caused some flooding in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media reports water getting into the first level of homes on Winkler Road and Cypress Lake Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer WSW steering flow was light across NE which enable sea breezes and river breezes to converge over inland areas over an unstable airmass. Cool mid level temperatures at 500 mb ranged from -10 to -12 deg C. This cool air aloft combined with high diurnal instability produced a few strong to severe storms with hail across NE FL. Strong WSW steering flow across SE GA produced a few strong wind gusts under stronger upper level dynamics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near the intersection of Old Jennings Road and Sedgwick Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Distant Hurricane Epsilon produced rough surf and strong rip currents along the east-central Florida coastline. A man and some family members were swimming at Daytona Beach when the group got caught in a rip current. All were able to return to shore except one man, who disappeared in the surf. His body washed ashore the following day, about nine miles north of where he went missing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 27-year old man from Ocala, Florida was swimming in rough surf with other family members at an unguarded portion of Daytona Beach during the early afternoon, when they got caught in a rip current. All family members except the 27-year old man returned safety to shore. The man disappeared in the surf and his body was recovered the following day about nine miles north of where he went missing. He died from drowning, after being caught in the rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted in St. Cloud, extending from a towering cumulus cloud. There was no rain in the vicinity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several citizens observed a funnel cloud over the Lake Tohopekaliga area, from Osceola Heritage Park and St. Cloud Lakefront Park. Photographs confirmed the funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong seabreeze thunderstorms produced high winds and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aviation business near RSW reported a wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved across the local area during the pre-dawn hours. Dynamical forcing was strong including deep layer shear and low level helicity values supportive of strong downburst wind and isolated tornadoes, but instability was overall lacking across the local area to support a widespread severe storm threat. A nose of instability just ahead of the squall line edged inland across portions of the Suwannee River Valley during the early morning hours, which phased with stronger dynamics with the squall lien and produced a few strong to severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and some blocked roads along and near NW 39th Ave. There was a heavy concentration of downed trees near the 2700 block of NW 39th Street. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust, stationary trough over the Gulf of Mexico kept deep, south/southwest flow and rich tropical moisture over South Florida. Several short waves rotating |around the base of the trough enhanced the showers and |thunderstorms that produced a tornado over the Golden Beach area in Miami-Dade from a waterspouts and flooding from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landfalling waterspout produced from a thunderstorm moved briefly onshore in Golden Beach as a tornado for approximately 3 minutes before lifting right before the Broward County line. The tornado moved northward at approximately 10 mph along the ocean side of Ocean Boulevard/Highway A1A from the 400 block of Ocean Boulevard to |near County Line Road. Damage consisted primarily of numerous broken/snapped tree branches, including to a large Sea Grape tree, a couple of newly-planted trees toppled, several damaged/twisted metal gates, and tossed lawn/patio furniture. Some of the debris ended up in the adjacent Atlantic Ocean. Most of the damage was confined to ocean-facing homes and properties. One home had water blown in through a set of sliding glass doors due to the force of the wind.||This damage is mainly consistent with EF-0 intensity, although one or two spots could have experienced winds close to the EF-1 threshold (80-85 mph). ||Thankfully, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust, stationary trough over the Gulf of Mexico kept deep, south/southwest flow and rich tropical moisture over South Florida. Several short waves rotating |around the base of the trough enhanced the showers and |thunderstorms that produced a tornado over the Golden Beach area in Miami-Dade from a waterspouts and flooding from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video on Twitter shows street flooding on 45th Street near Australian Ave. Surrounding area received around 3 to 5 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of strong upper level troughs pushed through the southeast states, resulting in another enhanced risk of severe storms from the Storm Prediction Center.  The tri-state area was hit fairly hard again, with numerous reports of wind damage documented along with several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial damage was noted near the intersection of Owen Place and Piney Grove Roads.  From the touchdown point, damage, consisting of hundreds of snapped and uprooted trees, was continuous right to the Jackson County border along Corbin Rd.  The tornado|intensified as it moved across Jackson County.  In addition to downing many hundreds of trees there was significant structural damage at two locations.  The first was at a farm off Farren Ranch Road where a two story outbuilding/home was completely|destroyed with debris thrown several hundred yards.  The second was along Bethlehem Rd. south of Kynesville Rd. where several mobile homes suffered significant damage.  One of the mobile homes was completely destroyed with it's frame wrapped into a tree. |Fortunately the mobile home was vacant at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust, stationary trough over the Gulf of Mexico kept deep, south/southwest flow and rich tropical moisture over South Florida. Several short waves rotating |around the base of the trough enhanced the showers and |thunderstorms that produced a tornado over the Golden Beach area in Miami-Dade from a waterspouts and flooding from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video on social media shows roads partially submerged in the Prospect Park neighborhood of West Palm Beach from heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts in the surrounding area were in the 3 to 5 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure centered over the Appalachians gradually weakened and shifted southward which relaxed the pressure gradient over the region and weakened the strong |northeasterly wind event into the evening. Breezy northeasterly winds continued along the coast with gusts up to 30-35 mph. Coastal hazards including coastal flooding, high surf, and high risk of rip currents continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0730, Emergency Management reported water inundation up to 2 ft in some areas of Flagler Beach, particularly along South Flagler Avenue from 15th Street, and locations southward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure centered over the Appalachians gradually weakened and shifted southward which relaxed the pressure gradient over the region and weakened the strong |northeasterly wind event into the evening. Breezy northeasterly winds continued along the coast with gusts up to 30-35 mph. Coastal hazards including coastal flooding, high surf, and high risk of rip currents continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1708, Emergency Management reported water over-flowing the banks of Pottsburg Creek near Beach Blvd. Flood water had reach the door of a sporting goods store. The USGS tide gauge at Pottsburg Creek measured a high tide of 2.25 ft MHHW datum, in the moderate flood stage category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled weak front extending across inland SE GA with a mid/upper level short wave trough moving over the boundary in the afternoon. Southerly steering over a very  moisture airmass with PWATs near 2.25 inches. The combination of the high moisture, weak SSW steering flow and lift under the passing upper level wave triggered locally heavy rainfall and gusty downdraft winds in sea breeze collisions storms toward the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1625, a trained storm spotter reported heavy rain caused street flooding up to 1 ft deep in some areas in the vicinity of St. Augustine. Roads impacted by flood water included State Road 312, State Road 207 and US Route 1. Cars were stalled in flood water. At 1730, Emergency Management reported roads were closed in St. Augustine due to flood water. Road closures included San Sebastian Bridge and King Street from Ponce de Leon Blvd to past Whitney Street.

Southerly steering over a very  moisture airmass with PWATs near 2.25 inches. The combination of the high moisture, weak SSW steering flow and lift under the passing upper level wave triggered locally heavy rainfall and gusty downdraft winds in sea breeze collisions storms toward the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. Augustine airport AWOS measured 2.80 inches within 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave, turned tropical depression, moving westward across the western Caribbean Sea abruptly formed into an intense Category 4 hurricane before making landfall over western Nicaragua on November 3rd, with a minimum central pressure of 923 mb just before landfall, and a minimum central pressure of 940 mb at landfall. Eta then weakened into a Tropical Depression as it moved across Central America and before it re-entered the western Caribbean Sea. Eta maintained Tropical Depression status for a few days before restrengthening into a Tropical Storm on November 7th, just west of the Cayman Islands. Tropical Storm Eta continued moving northward while intensifying into a strong Tropical Storm just before making landfall over Cuba early morning on November 8th. Tropical Storm Eta then began to move northwestward and towards FL on November 8th, with the center passing about 70 to 80 miles southwest of Miami, Florida and about 100 to 110 miles southeast of Naples, Florida. Strong Tropical Storm Eta made landfall on Lower Matecumbe Key in the Florida Keys with maximum winds estimated to be near 65 mph and a minimum central pressure of 991 mb. ||As Tropical Storm Eta continues to move westward into the Gulf of Mexico, it maintained Tropical Storm intensity. The lowest surface pressure recorded over South Florida was 1001.3 mb at Homestead Air Reserve with maximum sustained winds of 61 mph over land at Cache, located in west/central Miami-Dade County, during the early morning hours on November 9th. Recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts were measured across South Florida ranging from 45 to 70 mph. Tropical Storm Eta then meandered offshore southwest Florida the next couple of days before making landfall yet again near Cedar Key, Florida on November 12th.||Although the effects from Tropical Storm Eta were generally minor for wind and more significant for rainfall/flooding for South Florida, wind gusts of tropical storm force were measured across much of Palm Beach, Broward, Miami-Dade, and Collier counties. Rainfall flooding impacts were significant across South Florida, with around 4 to almost 21 inches across portions of Broward County, around 4 to almost 14 inches across portions of Miami-Dade County, and around 4 to almost 7 inches across portions of Collier and Palm Beach County. Almost  294,000 customers lost power during the event, most of them split between Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach counties. A peak storm surge of 3.39 feet was observed at Naples Pier, 2.26 feet at Virginia Key, and 1.95 feet at Port Everglades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of significant flooding received from the broadcast media, trained spotters, law enforcement, and NWS employees were received over the course of a day. Reports of significant street flooding and major, damaging rainfall in northern parts of the county, primarily in Miami Lakes, Country Club, Hialeah, Opa-Locka, and Miami Gardens where streets were impassable in many areas. Major flooding and impassable streets also occurred in downtown Miami and Brickell. Water entered structures in some of these areas, including Hialeah and Miami Gardens. Minor flooding was widespread county-wide. A number of stalled vehicles were reported as well. Rainfall amounts of 4 to almost 14 inches were measured across Miami-Dade County, with a little over 14 inches in Miramar, 8 to 10 inches near Hard Rock Stadium, and 5 to 9 inches in downtown Miami and Brickell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As TS Eta moved westward across the Florida Keys, rain bands produced maximum sustained winds generally between 30-40 mph across inland Miami-Dade County. A rain band moved across inland Miami-Dade County during the early morning hours of November 9th, with peak wind gusts of 61 mph/53 knots measured by the RAWS mesonet site at Cache (LPIF1) at 12:23 AM EST on November 9th. Additional wind gusts recorded of 52 mph/45 knots measured by the ASOS at Tamiami Airport at 09:53 PM EST on November 8th. A total of around 93,630 customers lost power in Miami-Dade County. Overall wind impacts were mainly confined to trees and power lines, with several concentrated areas of downed trees and poles along the barrier island communities as well as in deep south Miami-Dade County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably deep tropical moisture persisted across Martin and St. Lucie counties for nearly four days, just to the north of a stationary front. A secondary front then dropped south and stalled across Martin and St. Lucie counties. Moderate onshore flow within the moist air mass, combined with favorable conditions aloft, and resulted in development of several periods of heavy rain along the coast, with training of shower bands. Multi-hour rain rates of 1-to-3-inches-per-hour occurred during three seperate periods between the early morning hours of October 1 and late afternoon of October 3. ||Much of this same area was impacted by flash flooding during the early summer and the local water table remained very shallow, resulting in renewed flooding. Numerous roadways became impassable, drainage culverts were overwhelmed, standing water reached three feet or more in some neighborhoods and water entered several homes.||The most significant (flash) flooding affected portions of Martin County (primarily Hobe Sound, Port Salerno and Sewall's Point) on October 1 and 2. Considerable flooding of roadways, drainage canals and yards affected St. Lucie County (primarily Lakewood Park and the southern portion of Ft. Pierce), mainly on October 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 5 and 8 inches of rain fell during the early morning hours, within an area with a shallow water table due to previous heavy rainfall. Showers moved onshore or developed along the coast and trained over areas south of Port Salerno, impacting Hobe Sound most significantly, and in particular Hobe Heights. Rapid inundation occurred, with standing flood waters exceeding a depth of 3 feet in portions of Hobe Heights and surrounding neighborhoods, where flood waters approached homes, overtopped culverts and creeks and caused dozens of roads to become fully or partially impassable. The flood waters were very slow to recede.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 19 moved across the Everglades, and into the extreme southeast Gulf of Mexico, later strengthening into Tropical Storm Sally. Numerous rain bands and training convective cells produced flooding, particularly in Islamorada in the Upper Keys and in the Key West-Stock Island area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters, automated Citizen Weather Observing Program gauges and law enforcement reported heavy rain and flooding of the bayside of the Overseas Highway on Lower Matecumbe and the Old Highway on Upper Matecumbe, including stalled vehicles.  Rainfall measurements included through 1045 EST September 12th 11.00 inches since midnight and a nearby CWOP station with 8.61 inches since 417 EST September 12th on Lower Matecumbe Key.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 19 moved across the Everglades, and into the extreme southeast Gulf of Mexico, later strengthening into Tropical Storm Sally. Numerous rain bands and training convective cells produced flooding, particularly in Islamorada in the Upper Keys and in the Key West-Stock Island area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Key West Police Department reported numerous streets impassable due to flooding with stalled vehicles, including the Triangle (U.S. 1 and Roosevelt Boulevards), South Roosevelt and New Town neighborhoods along Northside Drive through Flagler Boulevard, and near Flagler and 5th Street.  Flooding later expanded to include the Bertha Street and Atlantic Boulevard neighborhoods, flooding a resident to a depth of 6 inches on Atlantic Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sally made landfall early on the morning of September 16 across Gulf Shores Alabama. The greatest impacts to the tri-state area came in the form of very heavy rain and significant flooding on and away from area rivers.  Record river flooding occurred on the Shoal River at Mossy Head as well as Holmes Creek at Vernon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few buildings were flooded near Highway 20 and Highway 65.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was hit by lightning and sustained minor smoke damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front was stalled near the FL-GA state line. High pressure was NE of the region. Onshore flow along and near the meandering front produced showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site measured 2.87 inches in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front was stalled near the FL-GA state line. High pressure was NE of the region. Onshore flow along and near the meandering front produced showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public mesonet site measured 2.71 inches within the past hour off of Renault Drive just south of Mayport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore flow over a moist airmass and weak ESE steering flow < 10 kts produced air mass storms with locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station measured 3.69 inches from 6:45 pm to 8:30 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High moisture content and stronger onshore easterly flow focused the afternoon sea breeze mergers toward the I-75 corridor in the afternoon and evening, which locally heavy rainfall and gusty winds occurred in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The JAX ASOS measured 2.58 inches in 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High moisture content and stronger onshore easterly flow focused the afternoon sea breeze mergers toward the I-75 corridor in the afternoon and evening, which locally heavy rainfall and gusty winds occurred in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along County Road 241. The time and cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical tropospheric trough (TUTT) moving northwest across the area along with a surface trough across the Florida peninsula allowed for scattered showers and storms to develop across the Atlantic waters and Biscayne Bay. Deep moisture and plenty of instability resided across the region as well, with PW's close to 2.5 inches. The overall flow regime was light and a little more southeasterly to southerly. Therefore, another day of increased diurnal coverage of showers and thunderstorms on top of already saturated soils with slow storm motion and high rainfall rates led to flooding across portions of Miami-Dade and Collier counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported via social media flooding produced by heavy rains from a thunderstorm near NW 36th Street and 24th Avenue with water half way up car tires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Atlantic surface high pressure, a mid-level trough pushing across the eastern United States, and a retreating warm front were the main features in play across South Florida. Increasing moisture ahead of a warm front that pushed northward allowed for efficient rainfall processes which led to flooding across portions of Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported 3 of 4 lanes impassable on NE 123rd Street, between North Bayshore Drive and Sans Souci Boulevard, due to street flooding caused by rounds of showers and storms. Water to around 1 foot deep in neighboring parking lots and some side streets. Water several inches deep on portions of northbound Biscayne Boulevard after the intersection with NE 123rd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Atlantic surface high pressure and elongated high pressure aloft at the mid-levels allowed for light southerly to southwesterly flow to prevail which allowed outflow boundaries to play a large part in convective focus. This allowed for training of heavy showers and storms which led to flooding across portions of Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flooding along NE 15th Street between Bayshore Drive and Biscayne Boulevard just north of downtown Miami. The flooding was caused by a cluster of heavy showers and storms with nearby rainfall measurements around 2 inches an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the full moon approached, spring tides made a return to coastal areas, with an increasing potential in coastal tidal flooding along east coast areas. This was further enhanced by a strong easterly wind component.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported water on portions of roadways, drives, and walks around times of high tide. Locations include Alton Road between North Bay Road and Pine Tree Drive and Collins Avenue between West 63rd Street and West 41st Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front extended from Hurricane Sally near the central Florida Gulf Coast to across NE FL and offshore of the local coast. This boundary combined with high tropical moisture and low level instability across the area combined produced a few strong storms in the afternoon, one with a brief tornado touchdown near Waycross.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed north of Argyle Forest Blvd and Cecil Commerce Center Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of showers and thunderstorms impacted the local area as the remnant circulation of Sally tracked farther inland and NNE of the region. Elevated low level winds of 35-40 kts mixed down in convection which produced gusty winds and some minor wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down near Birch Way and Birchtree Street in Palm Coast. The cost of damage was estimated for Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough in north Florida started pushing through the state during the two day period. This helped to increase afternoon and evening thunderstorm activity resulting in flooding in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HAM operator reports street flooding in Bradenton area with 4 inches of rain in a short amount of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough in north Florida started pushing through the state during the two day period. This helped to increase afternoon and evening thunderstorm activity resulting in flooding in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public works reports stream flooding that is causing some flooding on 37th street east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first strong onshore flow event of the season developed between strong high pressure building across the Mid-West toward New England and a frontal boundary SE of the area. Winds increased through the day as the pressure gradient strengthened. Sustained gradient winds and wind gusts were between 20-30 mph with gusts 30-40 mph, and a Wind Advisory was in effect for coastal counties. Waves of locally heavy rainfall moved onshore. A coastal flood warning was in place due to the stronger NE wind surge combining with seasonally high King Tides. Some areas with poor drainage and tidal estuaries had exacerbated flooding issues due to elevated tidal levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As of 1518, a public weather station measured a daily rainfall of 4.63 off of Cypress Links Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong NE Flow event peaked across the local area with sustained winds along the coast in the 25-35 mph range and observed gusts of 40-50 mph with the highest values along the NE FL coast. A wind advisory was issued. The combination of the strong onshore flow and seasonally high King Tides produced widespread moderate coastal flooding around high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1158, a NWS employee reported a foot of water at the intersection of State Road 16 and Lewis Speedway across the street from the San Sebastian River. At 1226,  the Emergency Manager reported significant coastal flooding around the Maria Sanchez Lake in downtown St. Augustine but homes were not impacted at the time. At 1226, a NWS employee reported significant inundation of flood water across several roads in Davis Shores. Water was across the entire roadway at the intersection of Anastasia Blvd and Moultrie (up to 1 ft deep in some areas). At 1830, the tide gauge at Racy Point on the St. Johns River measured a peak high tide of 2.24 ft MHHW datum. This was within the moderate flood category for the site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong NE Flow event peaked across the local area with sustained winds along the coast in the 25-35 mph range and observed gusts of 40-50 mph with the highest values along the NE FL coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1212, the tide gauge at Mayport measured a peak high tide of 2.44 ft MHHW datum. This was within the moderate flood level for this location. At 1324, the tide gauge at the Main Street bridge along the St. Johns River in downtown Jacksonville measured a peak high tide of 1.84 ft MHHW datum. This values was within the minor flood stage for this location.
