EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms that developed along the sea breeze collision became severe, producing large hail in Volusia County, and damaging winds in Volusia and Orange Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed reports of a roof awning that was blown onto the roof of an adjacent home in DeBary. Several trees were downed across the area, and a boat carport was blown across a yard. An additional report was received from Orange City where large tree branches were downed on Dixon Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along outflow boundary and sea breeze collisions. These storms produced two tornadoes in western Volusia County, including a rare August EF-2. In addition, other thunderstorms produced strong winds and hail along the Volusia and Brevard County coasts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed reports of quarter sized hail in South Daytona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud developed over Lake Toho in Kissimmee, then formed into a brief waterspout toward the soutwest portion of the lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather spotters and citizens observed development of a funnel cloud over Lake Tohopekaliga (Toho) in Kissimmee. The funnel cloud extended to the lake and became a brief waterspout toward the southwest portion of the lake, while moving little. A light shower was in the vicinity, with stronger thunderstorms farther east. The waterspout was visible from the northeast (St. Cloud) and from the west and southwest (Kissimmee). Multiple photographs and videos helped document the waterspout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure well to the north and hurricanes well east and well west of Florida resulted in several days of strong and gusty onshore flow. The long fetch of onshore winds also directed large northeast swells to the east-central Florida coast. In addition, large easterly swells associated with major Hurricane Teddy located near Bermuda propagated to the coast. These conditions combined to produce water levels 1.5 to 2.0 feet above predicted astonomical tides during 5-6 high tide cycles. Water levels measured at the Trident Pier CMAN at Port Canaveral peaked at 6.6 feet MLLW around 1000LST on September 20 and again around 1030LST on September 21. Minor to locally moderate coastal flooding occurred along several beachfront locations, mainly near high tide. Minor to moderate beach erosion was common along much of the coastline, with some areas of major erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate coastal flooding occurred near the times of high tide. The greatest impacts occurred as excess sea water entered St. Lucie Inlet and raised water levels within the St. Lucie River. Water inundated many roadways and properties adjacent to and near the river, primarily within Palm City, Stuart, Sewall's Point and Port Salerno. High water levels within the Indian River caused roadway flooding in Jensen Beach, as well as roadway flooding and water intrusion into a few homes in Hobe Sound. Sea water also entered Jupiter Inlet and raised water levels within the Indian River and Loxahatchee River, causing roadway flooding in far south Jupiter Island and in Tequesta. Beach erosion was primarily minor to locally moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of slow moving thunderstorms along the coast produced some flash flooding in the Apalachicola area as well as St. George Island with around 8 inches of rain measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported via social media that Avenue F along with adjacent yards were underwater in Apalachicola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of slow moving thunderstorms along the coast produced some flash flooding in the Apalachicola area as well as St. George Island with around 8 inches of rain measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported via social media that West Bay Shore Dr was underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary is draped over the Atlantic waters off the eastern sea board of the United States and across central Florida and into the Gulf of Mexico. Aloft, high pressure retreats into the central Gulf and western Caribbean with a mid-level trough amplifying across the peninsula. Additionally, a mesolow has developed along the frontal boundary off the Space Coast before pushing over the Lake Okeechobee region and into the Atlantic waters.  This setup combined with increasing amounts of available moisture |created areas of more vigorous convection that produced torrential rainfall that eventually lead to flooding across areas of Palm Beach and Hendry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported street flooding at the intersection of Forest Hill Boulevard and Southern Boulevard in Wellington, FL and vicinity due to passing storms. Rainfall estimates were around 2 inches within an hour.

In addition, the earthen berns surrounding several mosquito impoundments along both sides of the Indian River |suffered minor erosion from overwash. Beach erosion was mainly minor, except locally moderate along portions of Ft. Pierce Beach, where about 30 feet of beachfront was lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved between Grand Bahama and Southeast Florida in the morning of August 2. Isaias tracked north, moving nearly parallel to the eastern Florida coast over the next 24-36 hours, making its closest approach when the center was approximately 36 miles east-southeast of Fort Pierce Inlet. Outer rain bands on the western side of Isaias's circulation moved onshore of the east-central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds along the barrier islands, and gusts to tropical storm force west of the Intracoastal Waterway. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities. Tropical Storm Isaias produced minor beach erosion from elevated tide levels of approximately 1 foot above predicted tide values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Volusia County, wind gusts to tropical storm force in squalls occurred only along the immediate coast and for a short duration. Sustained winds in Volusia County remained below tropical storm force as the center of Isaias remained far away to the east. The highest sustained wind was measured at New Smyrna Beach (36 mph from the north at 0127LST on August 3), and the peak wind gust was also at New Smyrna Beach (41 mph from the north at 0124LST on August 3).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow-moving thunderstorm produced sub-severe wind gusts and isolated damage to a shed in Ocoee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pieces of a metal shed were torn off and blown off during a strong thunderstorm and carried a short distance down-wind. Power lines were also downed nearby. A weather spotter in the vicinity estimated peak winds of 50 to 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent thunderstorms produced heavy rain for several hours across far western Martin County, adjacent to Lake Okeechobee. Flooding of roadways, drainage canals and low-lying areas occurred, with water entering a fish camp.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent, heavy thunderstorms dropped between four and six inches of rain across far western Martin County between 1400 and 1700LST, as measured by two rain gauges. Roadways, drainage canals and low spots were flooded and water entered into the bar area of the J&S Fish Camp.

A total of around 2,000 customers lost power in Collier County. Overall wind impacts were minor and confined to trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure centered over New England will shifted south which continued strong northeasterly flow over the region. A more stable and drier airmass was in place which produced less widespread shower activity moving onshore compared to previous couple of days. Gusty northeasterly winds continued with the highest gusts 40-45 mph along the immediate northeast Florida coast. A wind advisory was in effect through the day for coastal northeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1800, Emergency Management reported tidal flooding between 19th and 25th streets in South Flagler Beach. Flood water threatened structures. Water was also reported over the road in North Flagler Beach between North 5th and 6th Streets. Tidal flooding impacted western Beverly Beach and also spilled over canals in Palm Coast and the Hammocks. Water flooded into garages, but there were no reports of intrusion into living spaces. Minor flooding was reported on Dead Lake  at the Fish Camp in the afternoon. High water covered the boat ramp and docks, but structures were not flooded. Also on this date, major beach erosion was reported along the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong NE Flow event peaked across the local area with sustained winds along the coast in the 25-35 mph range and observed gusts of 40-50 mph with the highest values along the NE FL coast. A wind advisory was issued. The combination of the strong onshore flow and seasonally high King Tides produced widespread moderate coastal flooding around high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1340, Emergency Management reported canals in Palm Coast were above the bulkheads. At 1515, Emergency Management reported tidal flood water covered Flagler Avenue at 23rd Street in Flagler Beach. At 1524, the tide gauge at Bings Landing on the Matanzas River measured a high tide of 2.87 ft MHHW datum. This value fell within the Moderate flood category for this station. At 1730, Emergency Management reported severe beach erosion along Jungle Hut Road were 4-5 ft of sand was missing. At 1928, a mesonet site measured a non-convective wind gust of 51 mph at Marineland from the ENE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest steering flow focus the dominant west coast sea breeze to converge with the east coast sea breeze during the afternoon and early evening east of the Highway 301 corridor toward the I-95 corridor. Convective ingredients including cool 500 mb temps of -10 to -11, ML Cape of near 1000 J/kg and a weak mid level short wave traversing the area overhead during peak sea breeze collisions created a few severe storms which produced large hail over NE FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to dime size hail was reported in Oceanway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms merged and became severe in Seminole County where several strong wind gusts were measured and damage to trees reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Orlando-Sanford International Airport, KSFB, measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph from the west.

Rainfall flooding impacts were moderate to significant across South Florida with around 3.5 to almost 10 inches across portions of Collier County, around 3 to almost 8 inches across portions of Miami-Dade County, and around 3 to almost 6 inches across portions of Broward County. Almost 4,000 customers lost power during the event, almost all of them split between Collier and Miami-Dade Counties. A peak storm surge of 1.43 feet was observed at Naples Pier and 1.16 at Virginia Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As TD Nineteen moved westward across South Florida and into the Gulf waters, rain bands produced maximum sustained winds generally between 25-32 mph. A rain band moved across inland Collier County during the early afternoon of September 12th into the early morning hours of September 13th, with a peak wind gust of 44 mph/38 knots measured by the RAWS mesonet OCOF1 at Ochopee at 4:03 PM EDT. A total of around 2,000 customers lost power in Collier County. Overall wind impacts were minor and confined to trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved between Grand Bahama and Southeast Florida in the morning of August 2. Isaias tracked north, moving nearly parallel to the eastern Florida coast over the next 24-36 hours, making its closest approach when the center was approximately 36 miles east-southeast of Fort Pierce Inlet. Outer rain bands on the western side of Isaias's circulation moved onshore of the east-central Florida coast producing sustained tropical storm force winds along the barrier islands, and gusts to tropical storm force west of the Intracoastal Waterway. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities. Tropical Storm Isaias produced minor beach erosion from elevated tide levels of approximately 1 foot above predicted tide values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Saint Lucie County, sustained tropical storm force winds affected the barrier islands, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county from early morning through late afternoon August 2. The highest sustained wind was measured at the Saint Lucie Nuclear Power Plant (41 mph from the northeast at 0705LST on August 2), and the peak gust was also at the Saint Lucie Nuclear Power Plant (46 mph from the northeast at 0705LST on August 2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout developed over Sykes Creek during a strong thunderstorm, then moved onshore a small portion of Merritt Island, producing weak EF-0 tornado damage to a home located immediately adjacent to the creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eyewitness reports indicated that a waterspout developed over Sykes Creek near a strong thunderstorm (however it was not raining at the time), then moved southeast and reached land less than a minute later. A NWS Storm Survey confirmed the circulation reached land toward the west end of Island Drive on North Merritt Island, where the strongest winds of the tornado occurred (EF-0, estimated winds 55-65 mph), removing a porch from the back yard of a home immediately adjacent to the creek. The tornado lifted the porch over the home and dropped it into the front yard. The tornado weakened as it traveled southeast for less than a half-mile, causing minor shingle, soffit and fence damage along it's short track.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Brevard County, sustained tropical storm force winds affected the barrier islands, with gusts to tropical storm force in squalls through much of the remainder of the county from late morning through very early morning August 3. The highest sustained wind was measured at US Air Force Wind Tower 0002 in Cape Canaveral (47 mph from the north-northeast at 2322LST on August 2) and the peak gust was at US Air Force Wind Tower 0108 in Cape Canaveral (53 mph from the northeast at 2000LST on August 2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms that developed along the sea breeze collision became severe, producing large hail in Volusia County, and damaging winds in Volusia and Orange Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail in Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With Tropical Storm Beta lingering over the northwestern Gulf of Mexico and Hurricane Teddy in the west/central Atlantic along with a frontal boundary off the eastern CONUS seaboard, clusters of convection developed that contributed to heavy rainfall, lightning, and strong gusty winds. With an increasing gradient flow and an increase in winds aloft, strong wind gusts mixed down with showers and thunderstorms over the course of several days along portions of the east coast and adjacent Atlantic waters. This in combination with astronomically high tides associated with the new moon led to elevated high tide cycles along the Atlantic coast and coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports from broadcast media of street flooding due to higher than normal tides. Areas include Hendricks Isle in Fort Lauderdale and portions of Las Olas Riverfront. Water was seen half way up parked car wheels, neighboring driveways, and overtopping docks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With Tropical Storm Beta lingering over the northwestern Gulf of Mexico and Hurricane Teddy in the west/central Atlantic along with a frontal boundary off the eastern CONUS seaboard, clusters of convection developed that contributed to heavy rainfall, lightning, and strong gusty winds. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported street flooding up to around curb level due to astronomical high tides near 30th Street, between Biscayne Blvd and Biscayne Bay.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports from broadcast media of coastal flooding along coastal portions of Palm Beach County, including water breaching sea walls and water flooding boat ramps and parking lots. Locations include areas along the Palm Beach Trail and at Currie Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system, turned tropical disturbance, moving across the western Atlantic and over the Bahamas abruptly formed into Tropical Depression Nineteen on September 11th. Tropical Depression Nineteen gradually continued westward and the center passed about 10 to 20 miles SSE of Miami, Florida during the morning of September 12th with a minimum central pressure of 1007 mb and maximum sustained winds of 35 mph. As Tropical Depression Nineteen moved westward over the South Florida peninsula, it continued to strengthen into a Tropical Storm known as Tropical Storm Sally. Lowest surface pressure recorded over South Florida was 1002.9 mb at Naples Municipal Airport and Marco Island Airport and maximum sustained winds of 35 mph over land at FAU Boca Raton Campus on September 12. Recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts were measured across South Florida ranging from 40 to 55 mph.||Although the effects from Tropical Depression Nineteen were generally minor for wind and more moderate to significant for rainfall/flooding for South Florida, wind gusts of tropical storm force were measured across much of Palm Beach, Broward, Miami-Dade, and Collier counties. Rainfall flooding impacts were moderate to significant across South Florida with around 3.5 to almost 10 inches across portions of Collier County, around 3 to almost 8 inches across portions of Miami-Dade County, and around 3 to almost 6 inches across portions of Broward County. Almost 4,000 customers lost power during the event, almost all of them split between Collier and Miami-Dade Counties. A peak storm surge of 1.43 feet was observed at Naples Pier and 1.16 at Virginia Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several broadcast media reports of significant street flooding from Coconut Grove to Brickell and Downtown Miami, and possibly extending into Little Havana. U.S. Highway 1 in Coconut Grove and Brickell was covered in water up to knee-high in places, leading to a number of stalled vehicles.||Rainfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches in about 6 hours were measured in the Coconut Grove and Little Havana areas, with 3 to 4 inches measured during a similar time frame in Downtown Miami and Brickell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta meandered in the southeastern Gulf of Mexico, along with expanding rain bands and lightning activity emanating from it. Scattered showers and storms made their way to the east coast over the course of the day and produced heavy rainfall as deep tropical moisture engulfed the region and clearing skies allowed for instability to grow. The area was already super saturated from Eta passing a day or so prior, so any rainfall can easily lead to flooding. In fact, flooding occurred in portions of Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Town of Jupiter reported flooding of roadways north of Old Dixie Highway, north of Center Street, due to heavy rainfall from showers and thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave, turned tropical depression, moving westward across the western Caribbean Sea abruptly formed into an intense Category 4 hurricane before making landfall over western Nicaragua on November 3rd, with a minimum central pressure of 923 mb just before landfall, and a minimum central pressure of 940 mb at landfall. Eta then weakened into a Tropical Depression as it moved across Central America and before it re-entered the western Caribbean Sea. Eta maintained Tropical Depression status for a few days before restrengthening into a Tropical Storm on November 7th, just west of the Cayman Islands. Tropical Storm Eta continued moving northward while intensifying into a strong Tropical Storm just before making landfall over Cuba early morning on November 8th. Tropical Storm Eta then began to move northwestward and towards FL on November 8th, with the center passing about 70 to 80 miles southwest of Miami, Florida and about 100 to 110 miles southeast of Naples, Florida. Strong Tropical Storm Eta made landfall on Lower Matecumbe Key in the Florida Keys with maximum winds estimated to be near 65 mph and a minimum central pressure of 991 mb. ||As Tropical Storm Eta continues to move westward into the Gulf of Mexico, it maintained Tropical Storm intensity. The lowest surface pressure recorded over South Florida was 1001.3 mb at Homestead Air Reserve with maximum sustained winds of 61 mph over land at Cache, located in west/central Miami-Dade County, during the early morning hours on November 9th. Recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts were measured across South Florida ranging from 45 to 70 mph. Tropical Storm Eta then meandered offshore southwest Florida the next couple of days before making landfall yet again near Cedar Key, Florida on November 12th.||Although the effects from Tropical Storm Eta were generally minor for wind and more significant for rainfall/flooding for South Florida, wind gusts of tropical storm force were measured across much of Palm Beach, Broward, Miami-Dade, and Collier counties. Rainfall flooding impacts were significant across South Florida, with around 4 to almost 21 inches across portions of Broward County, around 4 to almost 14 inches across portions of Miami-Dade County, and around 4 to almost 7 inches across portions of Collier and Palm Beach County. Almost  294,000 customers lost power during the event, most of them split between Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach counties. A peak storm surge of 3.39 feet was observed at Naples Pier, 2.26 feet at Virginia Key, and 1.95 feet at Port Everglades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak water level of 2.29 feet above mean higher high water was observed at Naples Pier at 11:06 AM EST on November 11th, with a peak surge of 3.39 feet above the predicted astronomical tide. Inundation was generally between 1-2 feet. Main impacts from storm surge was flooding along intracoastal waterways, with water overtopping sea walls and over docks along much of the Collier County coastline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Tropical Storm Eta continues to approach the FL coast in the NE Gulf of Mexico, bands of trailing showers and embedded thunderstorms spread across South Florida from the SSW to the NNE. Some of these bands contained heavy rain and gusty winds. With generally SSW steering flow in place, brief periods of training convection occurred. Due to super saturated antecedent conditions, minimal rain contributed to flooding across the east coast of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of flash flooding in Biscayne Park and North Miami were received. Roads were impassable in Biscayne Park with water entering crawlspaces and homes. Streets were also impassable at the intersection of Biscayne Boulevard and Sans Souci Boulevard in North Miami. Rain gauges in the area measured 3-4 inches of rain, most of it over the course of a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms that developed along the sea breeze collision became severe, producing large hail in Volusia County, and damaging winds in Volusia and Orange Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail to the size of golf balls in Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sally made landfall early on the morning of September 16 across Gulf Shores Alabama. The greatest impacts to the tri-state area came in the form of very heavy rain and significant flooding on and away from area rivers.  Record river flooding occurred on the Shoal River at Mossy Head as well as Holmes Creek at Vernon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at Beach Drive and Cherry Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid/upper level ridge was over the coastal Carolinas with Tropical Storm Sally tracking northward across the eastern Gulf of Mexico. Stronger easterly flow between these two system produced gusty gradient winds along the coast near 35-40 mph at times through the day. Inland thunderstorms formed over a moist and unstable environment over NE FL, with a few strong storms producing minor wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a home along SE 25th Terrace. The time of damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid/upper level ridge was over the coastal Carolinas with Tropical Storm Sally tracking northward across the eastern Gulf of Mexico. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Jim Taylor Field measured a wind gust of 46 mph. This event was given minimal damage as it corresponds to a wind damage report in Ocala just prior to this wind speed measurement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the area ahead of a cold front on Christmas Eve.  Limited instability precluded a more widespread damaging wind event, but there were still pockets of wind damage consisting of trees and power lines blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on E King St near Airport Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta moved north across the eastern Gulf of Mexico, parallel to the western Florida coast throughout the day on November 11. Eta then turned northeast and made landfall along the southern Florida Big Bend, and moved rapidly across North Florida in the morning of November 12. Outer rain bands on the southern side of Eta's circulation moved across Lake County producing a few tropical storm force wind gusts. There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from Eta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Lake County, sustained winds remained below tropical storm force with brief gusts to tropical storm force occurring in outer rain bands. The highest sustained wind measured was at the Leesburg Airport (31 mph from the south at 0532LST on November 12), and the peak wind gust measured was also at the Leesburg Airport (44 mph from the south at 0531LST on November 12).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pre-frontal squall line crossed the local area during the afternoon and into the early evening. Despite the limited instability, strong bulk shear of 50-60 kts and a strong low level jet produced widespread wind damage as a squall line crossed the local area. Wind gusts of 45-66 mph produced widespread tree and power line damage. Three QLCS tornadoes produced damage across NE FL where better surface based instability occurred during the afternoon ahead of the squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Fellowship Drive in Palm Coast. The time of damage was bsed on radar. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient was across the area as Tropical Cyclone Eta tracked NW an W across the FL Keys and over the eastern Gulf of Mexico. Strong onshore flow produced wind gusts of 40-50 mph along the coast. Waves of showers continued to move onshore across the local area, with locally heavy rain at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tidal flooding was reported in Flagler Beach along portions of South Flagler Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta offshore of the SW FL Gulf Coast tracked NE through the day, crossing NE FL while gradually weakening through the day. Strong, gusty winds and locally heavy rainfall impacted NE FL as bands of heavy showers moved onshore. The storm moved offshore of the NE FL coast in the afternoon, with improving local conditions over night. Elevated tides combined with persistent, strong onshore flow caused isolated minor flooding within the St. Johns River basin during times of high tide. Water levels were generally near or just below 1.5 feet MHHW datum at high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimal damage was reported across Marion county. The strongest wind measured at the Ocala Airport was 24 mph (190 deg) on 11/12 at 5:51 am. The peak gust was 36 mph (200 deg) on 11/12 at 5:40 am. On 11/12 at 5:20 am, a spotter reported small tree limbs were blown down at the Oakhurst Apartments. The most rainfall of the event for the local area was measured 5 miles east of Belleview of 1.23 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta offshore of the SW FL Gulf Coast tracked NE through the day, crossing NE FL while gradually weakening through the day. Strong, gusty winds and locally heavy rainfall impacted NE FL as bands of heavy showers moved onshore. The storm moved offshore of the NE FL coast in the afternoon, with improving local conditions over night. Elevated tides combined with persistent, strong onshore flow caused isolated minor flooding within the St. Johns River basin during times of high tide. Water levels were generally near or just below 1.5 feet MHHW datum at high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage was not reported across St. Johns county. On 11/12 at 6:53 am, the Weatherflow sensor at Summerhouse in Crescent Beach measured a peak wind of 30 mph (146 deg) with a peak gust of 38 mph (145 deg) at 6:33 am. On 11/12 at 6:54 am, the AWOS at the St. Augustine Airport measured at peak wind of 25 mph (160 deg) with a peak gust of 40 mph (160 deg) at 7:39 am. At 7:01 am, the Weatherflow sensor at Lewis measured a peak wind of 36 mph (142 deg) with a peak gust of 45 mph (148 deg) at 7:38 am. At 7:40 am, the CMAN Station at St. Augustine measured a peak wind of 40 mph (160 deg) with a peak wind gust of 54 mph (160) at 7:48 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Eta passed northwest through the middle Florida Keys on November 8 through 9, 2020.  Primary impacts from wind occurred as result of a primary rain band which drifted very slowly north through the Upper Florida Keys.  Peak wind gusts were mostly in the 50 to 60 mph range, downing trees and large tree limbs, utility lines, and a billboard. One residence was destroyed by a fallen tree resulting in 1 minor injury. Two other residential structures were significantly damaged, with three inaccessible  due to blocked streets. About 20 separate power outages affected about 1,000 customers, mostly in Key Largo. Storm Surge flooding was generally in the 1.5 to 2.5 foot range, producing significant street flooding in oceanside Key Largo neighborhoods.  The northbound right-of-way was damaged including major erosion, loss of riprap, and about 3,000 feet of fencing along northbound U.S. Highway 1 along the west end of Barnes Sound and Manatee Bay.  Minor overwash occurred in the middle Florida Keys at Key Colony Beach, along with yard and dock flooding along the Florida Bay side of Marathon. Widespread rainfall of 3.5 to over 8 inches was measured throughout the Keys, with the highest totals in Key Largo.  Damages were estimated near $500K due to mostly from homes damaged by falling trees and tree limbs, as well as utility damage. Storm Surge damage near $100K due to wave action on top of a minor storm surge to the U.S Highway 1 right-of-way.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most tropical-storm force wind gusts were minimal, under 45 mph, and intermittent  due to rain squalls.  As the circulation center of Tropical Storm Eta passed through the middle Keys, a short period of sustained winds of 31 to 33 mph with gusts near 45 mph occurred in Marathon and Crawl Key.  The peak gust of 46 mph was measured at a meso-network station on Crawl Key.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave, turned tropical depression, moving westward across the western Caribbean Sea abruptly formed into an intense Category 4 hurricane before making landfall over western Nicaragua on November 3rd, with a minimum central pressure of 923 mb just before landfall, and a minimum central pressure of 940 mb at landfall. Eta then weakened into a Tropical Depression as it moved across Central America and before it re-entered the western Caribbean Sea. Eta maintained Tropical Depression status for a few days before restrengthening into a Tropical Storm on November 7th, just west of the Cayman Islands. Tropical Storm Eta continued moving northward while intensifying into a strong Tropical Storm just before making landfall over Cuba early morning on November 8th. Tropical Storm Eta then began to move northwestward and towards FL on November 8th, with the center passing about 70 to 80 miles southwest of Miami, Florida and about 100 to 110 miles southeast of Naples, Florida. Strong Tropical Storm Eta made landfall on Lower Matecumbe Key in the Florida Keys with maximum winds estimated to be near 65 mph and a minimum central pressure of 991 mb. ||As Tropical Storm Eta continues to move westward into the Gulf of Mexico, it maintained Tropical Storm intensity. The lowest surface pressure recorded over South Florida was 1001.3 mb at Homestead Air Reserve with maximum sustained winds of 61 mph over land at Cache, located in west/central Miami-Dade County, during the early morning hours on November 9th. Recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts were measured across South Florida ranging from 45 to 70 mph. Tropical Storm Eta then meandered offshore southwest Florida the next couple of days before making landfall yet again near Cedar Key, Florida on November 12th.||Although the effects from Tropical Storm Eta were generally minor for wind and more significant for rainfall/flooding for South Florida, wind gusts of tropical storm force were measured across much of Palm Beach, Broward, Miami-Dade, and Collier counties. Rainfall flooding impacts were significant across South Florida, with around 4 to almost 21 inches across portions of Broward County, around 4 to almost 14 inches across portions of Miami-Dade County, and around 4 to almost 7 inches across portions of Collier and Palm Beach County. Almost  294,000 customers lost power during the event, most of them split between Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach counties. A peak storm surge of 3.39 feet was observed at Naples Pier, 2.26 feet at Virginia Key, and 1.95 feet at Port Everglades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As TS Eta moved westward across the Florida Keys, rain bands produced maximum sustained winds generally between 30-40 mph across metro Miami-Dade County. An earlier rain band moved across metro Miami-Dade County during the evening hours of November 8th, with peak wind gusts of 47 mph/41 knots measured by the ASOS at Homestead Air Reserve at 10:28 PM EST on November 8th. Additional wind gusts recorded of 49 mph/43 knots measured by the ASOS at Opa Locka Airport at 12:15 AM EST on November 9th, and 51 mph/44 knots measured by the ASOS at Miami International Airport at 11:33 PM EST on November 8th. A total of around 93,630 customers lost power in Miami-Dade County. Overall wind impacts were mainly confined to trees and power lines, with several concentrated areas of downed trees and poles along the barrier island communities as well as in deep south Miami-Dade County.

A peak wind gust of 40 mph/35 knots was measured at Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge at 10:32 PM on November 8th. Overall wind impacts were minor and mainly confined to trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave, turned tropical depression, moving westward across the western Caribbean Sea abruptly formed into an intense Category 4 hurricane before making landfall over western Nicaragua on November 3rd, with a minimum central pressure of 923 mb just before landfall, and a minimum central pressure of 940 mb at landfall. Eta then weakened into a Tropical Depression as it moved across Central America and before it re-entered the western Caribbean Sea. Eta maintained Tropical Depression status for a few days before restrengthening into a Tropical Storm on November 7th, just west of the Cayman Islands. Tropical Storm Eta continued moving northward while intensifying into a strong Tropical Storm just before making landfall over Cuba early morning on November 8th. Tropical Storm Eta then began to move northwestward and towards FL on November 8th, with the center passing about 70 to 80 miles southwest of Miami, Florida and about 100 to 110 miles southeast of Naples, Florida. Strong Tropical Storm Eta made landfall on Lower Matecumbe Key in the Florida Keys with maximum winds estimated to be near 65 mph and a minimum central pressure of 991 mb. ||As Tropical Storm Eta continues to move westward into the Gulf of Mexico, it maintained Tropical Storm intensity. The lowest surface pressure recorded over South Florida was 1001.3 mb at Homestead Air Reserve with maximum sustained winds of 61 mph over land at Cache, located in west/central Miami-Dade County, during the early morning hours on November 9th. Recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts were measured across South Florida ranging from 45 to 70 mph. Tropical Storm Eta then meandered offshore southwest Florida the next couple of days before making landfall yet again near Cedar Key, Florida on November 12th.||Although the effects from Tropical Storm Eta were generally minor for wind and more significant for rainfall/flooding for South Florida, wind gusts of tropical storm force were measured across much of Palm Beach, Broward, Miami-Dade, and Collier counties. Rainfall flooding impacts were significant across South Florida, with around 4 to almost 21 inches across portions of Broward County, around 4 to almost 14 inches across portions of Miami-Dade County, and around 4 to almost 7 inches across portions of Collier and Palm Beach County. Almost  294,000 customers lost power during the event, most of them split between Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach counties. A peak storm surge of 3.39 feet was observed at Naples Pier, 2.26 feet at Virginia Key, and 1.95 feet at Port Everglades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As TS Eta moved westward across the Florida Keys and towards the Gulf of Mexico, rain bands produced maximum sustained winds generally between 25-35 mph across inland Collier County, with gusts of 45-55 mph. A wind gust of 53 mph/46 knots was measured by a mesonet site in Ochopee at 2:32 PM EST on November 11th. A total of around 2,560 customers lost power in Collier County. Overall wind impacts were mainly confined to trees and power lines, with several concentrated areas of downed trees and poles along the barrier island communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pre-frontal squall line crossed the local area during the afternoon and into the early evening. Despite the limited instability, strong bulk shear of 50-60 kts and a strong low level jet produced widespread wind damage as a squall line crossed the local area. Wind gusts of 45-66 mph produced widespread tree and power line damage. Three QLCS tornadoes produced damage across NE FL where better surface based instability occurred during the afternoon ahead of the squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across State Road 121 at 13490 N SR 121 near the weigh station. It blocked both lanes. A car crashed into the tree, but no injuries occurred. Occupants were trapped inside due to a power line on the vehicle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down just SW of Stark around 6:16 pm. The vortex damaged trees, fences, and roofs on manufactured homes along its 4 mile path. Four people were displaced on Christmas Eve by the tornado. Two people were taken to the hospital as a precaution and later released. The tornado was 100 yards at its widest point with peak winds of 105 mph.

Rain gauge measurements in the area range from 1.5-4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 28 over the western Caribbean, which eventually became Hurricane  Zeta, helped to provide copious tropical moisture over South Florida. As an upper level trough traversed aloft, additional deep moisture overspread the South Florida ahead of an approaching frontal boundary. This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the broadcast media reported flooding via social media at the intersection of 6th Ave South and Congress Ave in Lake Worth. Two right lanes are seen flooded with water rising over the curb and is about 2 inches deep on the sidewalk. Cars are moving very slowly through the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure persisted NE of the local area with a strong surge of NE flow across the local waters through Oct 17th through the 19th.  The strong onshore flow combined with seasonally high King tides produced tidal flooding over the course of 3 days, with the peak of the event Monday Oct 19th. Widespread Minor tidal flooding impacted the Atlantic coast and locations within the St. Johns River basin with a few locations peaking at Moderate tidal flooding along the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak high tide was 1.65 MHHW datum at the Pottsburg Creek tide gauge at Beach Blvd. Minor tidal flooding begins at 1.5 ft MHHW at this location.

This resulted in widespread rainfall over several days that lead to significant flooding across the east coast metro areas, which had already received several inches of rainfall from previous days. ||The most impactful flooding occurred in Palm Beach County where several neighborhoods across the county experienced major street and field flooding, and several homes had water intrusion. The flooding lingered for several days in some of these areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported ponding of water on roadways around Sawgrass Mills Mall in Sunrise due to heavy rainfall. Nearby rain gauge in the area recorded 3.67 inches of rain from midnight to 7:49 am.

Flood waters lingered for several days in the area (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood).

Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall over already saturated soils resulted in flooding across portions of Broward and Palm Beach.

Flood waters lingered for over 24 hours (this is detailed in a separate storm entry under Flood).

Pictures show driveways and streets under several inches of water. Rain accumulations of 2-3 inches was measured in this area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ponding of water on Washington Road, just north of Southern Blvd and on South Flagler Drive. This is a combination of the high astronomical tide, which occurred around 10:40 am EDT and exasperated by rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Cristobal moved west of the Alabama coastal waters on the afternoon of June 7th, 2020. Cristobal made landfall in southeast Louisiana at 5 pm CDT on June 7th with estimated maximum sustained winds of 50 mph. Cristobal then tracked northwest into inland Louisiana late on the night of June 7th into the morning of June 8th. ||Cristobal did not produce sustained tropical storm winds across inland areas, sustained tropical storm force winds were limited to immediate coastal locations in southern Mobile and Baldwin counties. Inland of the immediate coast, some areas saw tropical storm force gusts. Damage was limited to very sporadic tree damage.||Storm Surge inundation peaked between 2 to 3.8 feet MHHW across coastal Alabama and Escambia County, FL. Moderate coastal flooding occurred across many coastal locations, particularly in and around the Mobile Bay region. Some roads were closed, including the west end of Dauphin Island, Highway 90 Causeway across Mobile Bay, Shell Belt/Coden Road, the foot of the Dog River Bridge and part of the Cochrane Causeway. More minor coastal flooding was observed further east across the western Florida panhandle.||Rainfall totals of 3 to 10 inches were recorded across southeast Mississippi, southwest Alabama and northwest Florida with the highest totals recorded in Mobile County and George County. The highest total was 10.33 inches in West Mobile.

Several vehicles appeared to be stalled on the roadside and temporarily unable to pass, along with the intersection of northwest 41st street and northwest 37th ave under water. Additionally, FDOT reported the two right lanes on SR-836 east beyond Lejeune Road were blocked due to the flooding. Public reported a rain gauge in the area had received 5 inches of rainfall in one hour.

There are several cars seen passing through, with others stranded along the side of the road. Several rain gauges in the area measured almost 5 inches of rain, most of which fell in about 2 hours between 1:30 PM and 3:30 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad upper-level troughing with southwesterly flow aloft allowed for deep tropical moisture to remain in place, while a northward lifting frontal boundary became the focus for showers and thunderstorms. Repeated rounds of heavy rainfall over already sufficiently saturated soils resulted in flooding across Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boynton Beach Police reported that Gateway Boulevard was closed due to flooding near Seacrest Boulevard. Nearby rain gauges measured around 4-5 inches of rain between 7 PM and 11 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy onshore flow brought hazardous marine and beach conditions to South Florida Atlantic coast beaches. At a beach in Palm Beach, two people had to be rescued after becoming caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Town of Palm Beach Lifeguards rescued two people from rip currents during the afternoon hours at Midtown Municipal Beach. The victims were extremely exhausted, but refused transport to a hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level troughing advanced eastward across the region, with a frontal boundary to the north, keeping South Florida well within an unstable airmass. Several perturbations aloft brought cooler temperatures that aided in the potential for stronger convection. As daytime heating increased, storms developed across Palm Beach. Lightning from one of these storms that struck a home, causing damage. Thankfully, no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house at the intersection of Lucky Lane and Southridge Road in Delray Beach. There was some charring to the roof and attic as well as electrical damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in association with a tropical wave extending from the western Caribbean into the Florida Straits. This tropical wave in concert with a departing shortwave, sparked convection across South Florida. Furthermore, brisk easterly flow aided a one storm in becoming strong enough to produce a strong wind gust in Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph (51 knots) was recorded at the Earthnetwork site WLLCH located at Wellington High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably deep tropical moisture persisted across Martin and St. Lucie counties for nearly four days, just to the north of a stationary front. A secondary front then dropped south and stalled across Martin and St. Lucie counties. Moderate onshore flow within the moist air mass, combined with favorable conditions aloft, and resulted in development of several periods of heavy rain along the coast, with training of shower bands. Multi-hour rain rates of 1-to-3-inches-per-hour occurred during three seperate periods between the early morning hours of October 1 and late afternoon of October 3. ||Much of this same area was impacted by flash flooding during the early summer and the local water table remained very shallow, resulting in renewed flooding. Numerous roadways became impassable, drainage culverts were overwhelmed, standing water reached three feet or more in some neighborhoods and water entered several homes.||The most significant (flash) flooding affected portions of Martin County (primarily Hobe Sound, Port Salerno and Sewall's Point) on October 1 and 2. Considerable flooding of roadways, drainage canals and yards affected St. Lucie County (primarily Lakewood Park and the southern portion of Ft. Pierce), mainly on October 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training bands of heavy showers redeveloped across coastal areas of Martin County during the morning of October 2 and persisted into the early afternoon. Rain totals of 5 to 8 inches fell across the same area impacted by 6 to 10 inches only a day earlier. Many of the same areas which experienced flash flooding were again inundated with rapidly rising water, exceeding 3 feet in some areas. High water resulted in the closing of dozens of roadways. Flood waters approached many homes and intruded into several homes resulting in damage. Drainage canals and creeks overflowed and high water from the Savannas Preserve flowed into surrounding areas. The flood waters were very slow to recede.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure with sufficient instability and a very strong low level jet affected the tri-state area.  A severe squall line crossed the area with numerous reports of wind damage.  A couple of tornadoes occurred in south-central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at County Road 2 and Cox Road.

Effects across the FL peninsula associated with the eastern periphery of the tropical storm included heavy rainfall, gusty winds and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding near US 301 and Gibsonton Drive, road barely passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of anomalously moist tropical air surged far northeast of Tropical Storm Cristobal, which was located over the Bay of Campeche. Numerous showers and thunderstorms redeveloped near the Martin County coast and trained across the eastern half of the county from the evening of June 2nd through the afternoon of June 3rd. High rainfall rates and excessive accumulations resulted in flood water intrusion into two dozen homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain totals of 5-10 inches affected portions of Jensen Beach, Stuart, Palm City and Hobe Sound between the evening of June 2 and mid afternoon on June 3. Excessive rain rates (6 inches in less than 4 hours) occurred within an area of saturated soils from Palm City to Hobe sound, with peak event total rainfall of 11 to 13 inches measured by a weatherstem site and a SKYWARN spotter, respectively, both in Hobe Sound. Fourteen homes experienced minor damage consisting of several inches of water accumulation and 8 homes sustained major damage with waters levels up to or beyond electrical outlets. Numerous roadways throughout the area became impassable due to high water and were closed. Additionally, water poured into some homes in Stuart near Manatee Pocket, where heavy rain combined with the high tide, likely causing water levels to rise along a stream connected to the St. Lucie River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought a round of showers and thunderstorms to the area.  One storm produced a tornado as it moved through Madison county with some damage, resulting in damage to farm buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado initially touched down near NW Little Cat Road in Madison County. Significant tree damage and minor structural damage was noted at a home along County Road 150. The tree damage intensified as the tornado moved northeast across a quail farm along Boggy Bay Road. Significant tree damage and damage to farm outbuildings suggests EF-1 intensity. This was the tornado's widest point. The path narrowed as it crossed County Road 253 where a significant number of snapped pine trees was noted. The tornado then weakened, with no other known structural damage, before lifting just southwest of State Road 53 near the Florida-Georgia line. Maximum winds are estimated to be 105 mph with a maximum width of 475 yards. Thanks to Madison County Emergency Management and Florida Division of Emergency Management for help with the survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped from the Carolina's through the northeastern Bahamas, with a zone of deeper moistures situated across South Florida. Prevailing east-southeasterly flow allowed for both the Gulf Atlantic sea breeze to progress inland through peak heating hours. Several pockets of cooler, drier air aloft worked in concert with steepening low-level lapses to produce sufficient accent for showers and thunderstorms to develop within the saturated airmass. Collisions between the sea breeze boundaries and additional outflow boundaries resulted in a few stronger storms that produced a funnel cloud and flooding across Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported a funnel cloud extending 80 percent of the way to the ground from cloud base in the Belle Glade area. Spotter noted that the funnel did not extend to the ground. This was associated with generating convection that interacted with a lake-breeze boundary peripheral to Lake Okeechobee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Cristobal developed in the southern Gulf of Mexico on June 1 before moving north and making landfall in southeast Louisiana on June 7. Effects across the FL peninsula associated with the eastern periphery of the tropical storm included heavy rainfall, gusty winds and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River overflowing with yard flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure draped across the region brought east to southeasterly flow. This pattern favors the development of both The Atlantic and Gulf sea breezes each afternoon. Additionally, a plume of Saharan dust was slowly trickling in from the Caribbean, bringing drier air to the midlevels, while the leading edge also acted as a pseudo elevated mixed layer, enhancing updraft strength enough to produce hail in a few stronger storms over Glades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brighton Reservation Police reported Nickel size hail (0.88 inches) and wind gusts up to 50 mph with storms over the Cane Hill area of the reservation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection initiated along the Atlantic and Gulf sea breezes as these boundaries pushed inland. Despite the presence of the Saharan Air Layer (SAL) over the region, several perturbations from the Bahamas allowed for an uptick in convective coverage as they moved across the area. The leading edge of SAL allowed for a few stronger storms to develop and produced hail across Broward and Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported hail between dime and nickel size from a strong thunderstorm about 1 mile NW from the intersection of Weston Road and Griffin Road. Pea size hailed was also reported by the public via social media near Emerald Estates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward retreating frontal boundary allowed for generally east to southeasterly flow to keep abundant moisture in place across South Florida. Morning thunderstorms developed over Miami-Dade, with lightning striking a home, causing some damage. Thankfully no one was injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An article from Local 10 News reported a house near the intersection of Bryon Ave and 92nd Street was struck by lightning from a thunderstorm that pushed across the town of Surfside in the morning.  Lightning struck a tall structure on the roof, blowing up the concrete, which landed in six or seven different places and even hit his SUV, though it only caused a few scratches. Thankfully, no one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-southwesterly flow allowed for deep tropical moisture to stay in place across South Florida. This prevailing flow also allowed the Gulf sea breeze to progress further inland, pinning the Atlantic sea breeze to the coast, becoming the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorm. As a mid-upper level shortwave rotated across the region aloft through the day, coverage of thunderstorms increased along the east coast and brought gusty winds and heavy rainfall that lead to flash flooding in Broward and Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed public reports of wind damage to homes and trees in the West Boca area. Several patios lost their roofs and large tree limbs were also downed. Wind gust estimated from a mesonet station east of the damage area at approximately 56 mph (47kt). Time also estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in Orange County produced wind damage to trees, as well as small hail and localized flooding. Another thunderstorm produced severe winds in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny sized hail east of Orlando on Goldenrod Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in Orange County produced wind damage to trees, as well as small hail and localized flooding. Another thunderstorm produced severe winds in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Orange County Fire Rescue reported a tree down near the intersection of Lake Margaret Drive and Conway Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in Orange County produced wind damage to trees, as well as small hail and localized flooding. Another thunderstorm produced severe winds in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Orange County Fire Rescue reported a tree down near the intersection of Curry Ford Road and Chickasaw Trail, and another tree down near Green Meadow Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in Orange County produced wind damage to trees, as well as small hail and localized flooding. Another thunderstorm produced severe winds in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Cape Canaveral Air Force Station Skid Strip, KXMR, measured a peak wind gust of 52 knots from the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in Orange County produced wind damage to trees, as well as small hail and localized flooding. Another thunderstorm produced severe winds in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorms produced between 4.5 to 5.5 inches of rain in the Lake Nona area from approximately 1900-2300 LST. The heavy rainfall caused localized flooding, and Orange County Fire Rescue reported that all six lanes of Narcoossee Road at Eagle Creek Sanctuary Boulevard in Lake Nona were closed due to high standing water. Six vehicles were stranded in the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced severe wind gusts as it impacted the Melbourne International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Melbourne International Airport measured a peak wind gust of 50 knots from the north-northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between a subtropical high in the Atlantic and Tropical Depression Cristobal over the Yucatan Peninsula helped to loft deep tropical moisture into South Florida. An outflow boundary racing northward from Cuba and strengthening southwesterly flow in the upper levels in association with an upper-level trough over the northern Gulf of Mexico provided sufficient lift and enough synoptic ascent for thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon and evening hours. With the abundant moisture in place, flash flooding occurred across Palm Beach. A Flood Watch was in Effect through 8PM EDT June 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports from the Town of Jupiter Police Dispatch of blocked, impassable streets and vehicles stranded in the 400 block of Hugh street and Near the Jupiter River Estates neighborhood. Social media posts by WPTV and CBS 12 in Palm Beach also showed half-foot of water on SR 811/Alt A1A in Jupiter and water completely covering Pittsburgh Drive in Jupiter, with water reaching about halfway up front yards. A total of 3 to 4 inches fell in that area, most of it in about an hour's time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms quickly intensified and produced quarter-sized hail in Osceola County and hail up to the size of golf balls, along with severe winds in Seminole County. A thunderstorm also produced small hail in Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny sized hail in Port Orange, near the intersection of Nova Road and Herbert Street, and near the intersection of Peninsula Drive and Dunlawton Avenue.

Scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms developed across Lake, Orange and Volusia counties producing areas of damaging winds and several tornadoes and waterspouts over inland lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped from the Carolina's through the northeastern Bahamas, with a zone of deeper moistures situated across South Florida. Prevailing east-southeasterly flow allowed for both the Gulf Atlantic sea breeze to progress inland through peak heating hours. Several pockets of cooler, drier air aloft worked in concert with steepening low-level lapses to produce sufficient accent for showers and thunderstorms to develop within the saturated airmass. Collisions between the sea breeze boundaries and additional outflow boundaries resulted in a few stronger storms that produced a funnel cloud and flooding across Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snap map image of poor-drainage-related flooding of residential property in the Belle Glade area, enhanced by runoff from earlier heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest easterly flow helped to keep the Gulf Breeze pinned along the coast. Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed as the Atlantic breeze moved inland and collided with the Gulf Breeze. A mid level trough situated along the the west coast and abundant moisture in place aided in producing robust updrafts that allowed several rounds of heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms over Naples, which resulted in Flash Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports from the public and social media of widespread street flooding in downtown Naples near the intersection of south 9th street and south 5th avenue through West Lake in Downtown Naples, where a sports utility vehicle was largely submerged into a storm-drain runoff area. The significant flooding extended northward along North Tamiami Trail, North of Naples Bay, where water was seen extending over street surfaces onto sidewalks and continuing across Quail Run Golf Club in Naples just south of Pine Ridge Road, where a few cars were flooded by becoming partially submerged in flood waters. Areas of flooding extended a couple feet deep over streets and surrounding property. A report from an automated rain gauge within Wunderground Network -- Site ID KFLNAPLE256, which is located near the intersection of Leeward Lane and Putter Point Drive recorded a total of 4.7 inches of rain that fell in association with heavy showers and thunderstorms over several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above normal levels of tropical moisture over Martin County allowed an area of heavy showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and move slowly across an area saturated from excessive rain which fell over previous days. Flooding quickly redeveloped, with water entering into several homes, mainly in the town of Hobe Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell within two hours, across an area which received 7 to 13 inches of rain over the previous three days. With saturated soils and standing water remaining in many areas, flooding quickly developed. Flood waters entered several homes, mainly within Hobe Heights. Some homes may have experienced water intrusion two days earlier from the first period of flooding. Numerous roadways within Hobe Sound became inpassable due to high levels of standing water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from trained spotters and the public in Grand Island indicated that straight-line winds estimated between 55-60 mph occurred at approximately 2010 LST. The damage began along the north shore of Lake Eustis where several medium to large tree branches were downed. Additional damage occurred on Bristol Circle and Ashley Court where several fences were blow over, small trees toppled, medium tree branches downed, and loose backyard items tossed into adjacent properties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving frontal boundary brought deep tropical moisture across South Florida. The abundant moisture translated into heavy rainfall across the east coast metro, where flooding materialized across areas in Palm Beach and Broward that received repeated rounds of showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snap map video of street flooding occurring near the intersection of US highway 1 and 13th avenue North in Lake Worth. Water had pooled to a depth of a few inches on street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-southwesterly flow allowed for deep tropical moisture to stay in place across South Florida. This prevailing flow also allowed the Gulf sea breeze to progress further inland, pinning the Atlantic sea breeze to the coast, becoming the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorm. As a mid-upper level shortwave rotated across the region aloft through the day, coverage of thunderstorms increased along the east coast and brought gusty winds and heavy rainfall that lead to flash flooding in Broward and Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported Flash Flooding from heavy rainfall that fell in about an hour on St. Andrews boulevard near Town Center Mall in Boca Raton that left several vehicles stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions became favorable for severe weather in central Florida due to enhanced winds aloft on the far eastern periphery of Tropical Storm Cristobal which was moving north over the central Gulf of Mexico. Scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms developed across Lake, Orange and Volusia counties producing areas of damaging winds and several tornadoes and waterspouts over inland lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous eyewitness reports, photographs and video were received from trained spotters and the public of a large waterspout that developed on Lake Monroe, which then came onshore briefly along the north shore of the lake as a rapidly weakening tornado. The waterspout developed on Lake Monroe at approximately 1927 LST and quickly became large, reaching an estimated maximum width of approximately 200 yards. The slow-moving waterspout then reached the north side of the lake near the 860th block of Lakeshore Drive in Enterprise at approximately 1934 LST as a rapidly weakening EF-0 tornado. The tornado dissipated between Lakeshore Drive and Jacob Brock Avenue, just east of the Florida United Methodist Children's Home and west of Broadway Street. An eyewitness near the point of dissipation, initially observed the waterspout over the lake, then described a complete lull of wind, followed by a increase of winds from the opposite direction, suddenly becoming very strong. Based on this report, along with only minor damage to trees near the lakefront, peak winds were estimated between 45 and 55 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey confirmed straight-line winds estimated at 55-65 mph impacted the Daytona Flea and Farmers Market in Daytona Beach around 2115 LST. Damage consisted of the removal of a large portion of a corrugated roof, with metal roofing peeled back on several adjacent buildings. Roof debris was blown downwind 100 yards onto adjacent portions of Interstate 95, causing the roadway to be closed for several hours. No other damage was noted in the area and there were no injuries or fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey and numerous eyewitness reports, photographs and video confirmed a waterspout/tornado moved north along a 5.1 mile path from Lake Conway to Thornton Park. The peak intensity was estimated at 100-105 mph.||A waterspout initially formed on the west-central portion of Lake Conway at approximately 1820LST and traveled north, briefly coming onshore and crossing a narrow strip of land at Hoffner Avenue, then moving back over water, crossing Little Lake Conway. The waterspout then moved back onshore as a tornado at the south end of Ferncreek Avenue. The tornado nearly paralleled Ferncreek Avenue and Martin Street as it moved nearly due north, crossing State Road 408, and lifting at Lake Lawsona Park at approximately 1832LST.||Damage was continuous along a 5.1 mile swath with a width of 350-500 yards. As the tornado came onshore at Hoffner Avenue it briefly attained EF-1 intensity (peak winds estimated at 85-90 mph) resulting in severe damage to the roof of a lakefront home and numerous large trees were toppled. Damage along much of the remainder of the path was rated EF-0 (winds estimated 65-85 mph).||The EF-0 damage consisted generally of uprooted trees, toppled trees, and snapped large branches. Several large trees fell onto homes, causing significant damage mainly to roofs. Multiple vehicles were damaged due to downed trees and fallen large branches. ||The tornado reached peak intensity of EF-1 in the vicinity of Ferncreek Avenue, Lake Margaret Drive and Carmia Drive (estimated peak winds 100-105 mph). Several one story apartment buildings lost significant portions of their roofs.||There were no injuries or fatalities. The Orange��County Property Appraiser's Office reported 42 homes and businesses were damaged, totaling $955,748.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper trough moved across the southeast states with numerous severe storms, including widespread wind damage and tornadoes.  The tri-state area was under an enhanced to moderate risk of severe storms from the Storm Prediction Center.  Some of the tornadoes were significant and rated EF2.  Unfortunately, one fatality occurred in southeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a home and some shingles were removed from the roof.  Additional trees were blown down in the yard.  Winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light east to southeasterly flow around the Atlantic Ridge allowed for both sea breeze circulations to develop in the afternoon. Despite ridging present aloft, ample low level moisture was in place for convection to initiate along the aforementioned boundaries. A few storms were able to become strong with the leading edge of the Saharan Air Layer (SAL) approaching the region, which acted as an elevated mixed layer. One of the stronger storms produced a funnel cloud along a boundary collision over Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off-duty NWS employee observed a funnel cloud -- possibly a pair of funnel clouds -- looking southeastward from the intersection of Florida Highway 700 and Everglades Street in Canal Point. The off-duty NWS employee observed the eastern funnel quickly elongate and assume a rope-type appearance before dissipating concurrent with the expansion of the peripheral rain shaft -- all while observing the visible cloud rotation and funnel base remaining well elevated above ground. A sheriff officer from South Bay also reported hail around half an inch and wind gusts around 50 mph along US 27 at Okeelanta associated with these storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men were struck by lightning and critically injured while fishing at Hobe Sound Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Martin County Fire Rescue and local broadcast media reported that two men were struck by lightning while fishing at Hobe Sound Beach. The lightning strike occurred at approximately 1530 LST, 200 yards north of a lifeguard tower. First responders arrived to find the two men injured and both were transported to local hospitals in critical conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports received from trained spotters of a funnel cloud that persisted for approximately 3 minutes near Lake Yale.

The tornado was on the ground less than one minute west of Emeralda Island Road between Lake Griffith and Lake Yale. The tornado produced no damage as it was over a marsh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was in place (PWAT 2.5) with the area on the east side of TS |Cristobal as the storm approached the LA Gulf Coast. Waves of moderate to heavy rainfall with isolated embedded thunderstorms pivoted northward across the local forecast area into the evening with a more solid band of moderate to heavy rainfall developing across north Florida as low level convergence increased between a lifting frontal zones across south-central  FL and a persistent low level ridge axis holding firm just north of the Altamaha River basin. This ridge will limit the northward extend/expansion of the convective band into Sun, and generally focused a W-E oriented swath of heavy rainfall generally near and south of the I-10 corridor overnight into Sunday morning on 6/7.  The ridge strengthened north of the Altamaha Region Sun afternoon 6/7, which shunt the corridor of heavy rain SSW toward the I-75 corridor of NE FL with drier mid level air (700-500 mb) decreasing rain chances across SE GA and eastern NE FL into Sun evening. In addition to heavy rainfall potential, there was a low tornado threat for NE FL into the evening of 6/6 with elevated 0-1 km shear values 20-25 kts with low level ESE winds under increasing  SSW winds out of the GOMEX, with speeds increasing to 35-45 kts at 850 mb through 06z Sun 6/7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak EF0 tornado with peak winds near 75 mph briefly touched down just north of New River and just south of SW County Road 18A where it produced structure damage including roof damage to a shed, a porch, gazebo, and a pump house. Trees were blown down at a property along SW 43rd Terrace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front was lingering across NE Florida to near the Golden Isles of SE Georgia where PWAT was near 2 inches. Diurnal instability and forcing near the front produced a few strong to pulse severe storms and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near intersection 441.
